D:OSII and BG3 are possibly the best cRPGs ever produced, Larian has certainly delivered the current peak for the genre. I play a lot of games, rarely do any of them stay interesting after 60+ hours, some barely make it to 30+. The depth of character builds, the excellent writing found throughout, the seamless world and interesting stories packed into every corner of it, the top-tier vocal and mocap performances, the intellectually rewarding combat system, the endless player freedom, it all comes together into a package so compelling that I absolutely had to play and replay until I experienced all of it. At 300+ hours I've seen every possible ending for every quest, tried every class and every difficulty, used every weapon, crafted and used every potion and scroll, and died thousands of times in every possible way. Even though I know the game like the back of my hand and I'm personally done playing solo runs (at least for a couple years), I'll still eagerly jump back in anytime a friend wants to play and still be surprised and delighted by the emergent situations that arise from something so intricately and thoughtfully designed. Larian has ruined all other RPGs for me, Bethesda's efforts (especially recently) pale in comparison, Obsidian's attempts somehow always feel incomplete, Owlcat's games feel bloated, and all the other indie and AA developers simply can't compete with the scale, polish, and overall quality that Larian has achieved.  B-B-BUT... "The turn-based combat is slow/hard!" "I heard its basically a dating simulator!" "There isn't enough loot!" "[Insert Game Here] should have won GOTY!" "It's not really BG3, Black Isle didn't make it!" "LGBT characters exist, that's woke!"  If you find yourself using one of these above excuses to hate this game you either: A. Are a child or other person without a fully developed frontal lobe. B. Never learned to read past a 6th grade level. C. Are repeating something you heard in a clickbait video created by someone in one of the previous categories.  Admit defeat, accept that there is simply no valid excuse not to play this game as a fully-formed adult, and give in to experiencing one of the best pieces of videogame design ever made. Sure it's not like other games you've played before, maybe it seems extremely difficult at first, these things are also true of every Souls-like ever produced and I don't hear anyone complaining about that, we only hear "git gud". There is no grinding your way to greatness like in Souls, you have to listen, read, understand, process, then think your way through scenarios. Once you start doing this you realize it is far more dynamic and interesting than the "swing sword, dodge, repeat" loop of most other RPGs. Okay so you died in some way you feel was unfair, instead of getting all up in your feelings you can read the combat log, check the stats of your opponents, optimize your positioning, experiment with new skills or gear, hammer out a better plan, and try again. Maybe you send one person to bait them into a chokepoint where the rest of your party lies in wait, maybe you climb up a cliff or into the rafters and rain down from above, maybe you charm or intimidate or bribe or lie your way through instead, or maybe you sneak past, maybe you go find a new spell or piece of gear that turns the tide, maybe try out some of those special arrows and potions you've been hoarding, maybe try turning the difficulty down (there is no shame), maybe you convince a gang of trolls to help, maybe you recruit a druid who can turn into an Owlbear and kamikaze body-slam them from the upper atmosphere, maybe you go get your eyeball replaced with a magical artefact, maybe you drink bloody sludge out of a Hag's well (there is no shame), maybe you reload back to before you did that, maybe learn to speak with animals and discuss with the local rat and bird population, maybe throw magic-draining flowers at them, maybe fill your brain with magic parasites that turn you into a tentacle monster but let you grow wings and shatter the psyche of the weak-minded, I could go on and on and on. If your creativity truly runs out then just respec and gear up someone for a high strength open-hand monk build and watch as your enemies get their cheeks clapped by a calisthenics expert in a dress. This game isn't about having faster reflexes or better precision, but having a bigger brain that can adapt to rapidly changing scenarios and produce creative solutions. If brain bad, game bad. If brain good, game great. Personally I can't find a hat that fits this gargantuan noggin.

One of the all time greats.   Larian knocked it out of the ballpark with this one (I had no doubt they would considering how great DOS 1 and 2 are). I've played through multiple times now on multiple difficulties both with and without mods. I'll probably keep playing it on and off for at least the next few years. Very much looking forwards to what Larian does next.  Fun, interesting, engaging, plenty of variety, plenty of player agency, solid writing, and the list goes on.   Lvl1-12 is optimal, if you've ever DM'd DnD5e the higher levels can be a mess so  I'm glad they didn't push it to 20.  Overall balance can be a bit wonky, especially on Honor Mode, but that plays both to and against the player. If you learn the system you can work with it, not a big problem. That said, I personally find that Honor Mode is a bit on the too hard side without any cheese or optimized builds which sadly narrows your options. I think this is a side effect of how a lot of tabletop dynamics can't be translated to a CRPG and there is a custom difficulty mode to tweak things anyways. That and DnD5e isn't exactly perfectly balanced in the first place.   Do note though this is a DnD inspired game that is absolutely not RAW. Think of DnD flavored DOS2 rather than Solasta. Many things that don't fit how DnD works were also in DOS2 so I think it's just a result of building on what they already had/knew. Some are understandable, although others are outright bad. If you're familiar with the DnD5e system you're going to run into some frustrations if you expect the game to mirror it (looking at how getting knocked prone is handled and Trickery Domain Clerics). Luckily with the mod support there are plenty of resources to mold the game to your liking.

From a Psychological point of view, I will state that, it is indeed an amazing game. Addictive, terrifyingly so. But I must say that for the entirety of Act 2 I am always miserable. The atmosphere, the backstories. Being a very immersive Role-Playing Game the decision making is already mentally draining for sensitive people (still amazing and addictive XD), The Act 2 is shorter than 1 and 3 but still very... VERY emotionally intense. I still recommend this game so much. It is truly one of a kind. But... do beware of Act 2. For really sensitive people I would recommend having a full guide for the exact outcome you want on hand, just to eliminate the energy drain of act 2. I have OCD and anxiety (On treatment), I'm the type of person that just to be sure I would get the exact outcome I wanted, I would auto-save before any damn decision and read to a fault every single outcome. More than once I had to stop gaming abruptly because of stress induced panic attacks. Still I adore gaming I'm just giving my opinion and recommendations to the sensitive people out there that still wanna enjoy an amazing story and this wonderfully developed game! Happy gaming! Stay safe ;)

I replayed this game after a failed first run (60-ish hours) and, boy, what a run it was.   Baldur's Gate 3 is, to put it simply, one of the best RPGs that I've ever played and, probably, one of the best RPGs ever made.   Some of the best things about Baldur's Gate 3:  [b]Writing[/b]: every line has its "weight", and feels perfect for the moment.  [b]Gameplay and combat system[/b]: freedom, freedom, freedom. You can do literally whatever you want. You have freedom to approach battles the way you prefer, but also the freedom to play as you like. Want to skip a big battle? There's a way. Want to convince some enemies not to fight or, better, off themselves? You can do it. As I said, you can do whatever you want.  [b]Great quests[/b]: an incredible main quest packed with great moments, but also plenty of well written side quests. Honestly, I found a lot of them just messing around, so explore!  [b]Exploring[/b]: yeah, about that... exploring is encouraged and rewarded, in the best way possible. Take your time to talk to NPCs and explore every corner of the map.    Is there something I didn't like in Baldur's Gate 3? Not much, honestly, but here is maybe my [i]least favourite[/i] aspect of the game:  [b]Some party members don't make sense[/b]: Jaheira and Minsc literally saved the world multiple times lore-wise, but in BG3 they become just as strong as you... not very plausible.    Baldur's Gate 3 is just... a great experience. Play it. Really.     9.5/10

Possibly the very best game that exists, within its specific genre of Story-heavy tactical CRPG. I won't go into a lot of details on why it's good, as a lot of other people have already done so. Instead, I'll share some thoughts I have that might not so widely shared.   If you struggle with analysis paralysis or making decisions, this is not be the game for you. There are a ton of decisions to make, both story-wise and character-wise. I often had to save mid-conversation and take a walk or sleep on it before I could make a decision on what to do.   An important factor to be aware of is that failures aren't necessarily bad in this game. The developers have included a lot of content that only occurs when you fail a check. As such, I highly recommend against save scumming to always succeed on all die rolls. I am highly susceptible to this myself, so I played on Honor mode to prevent myself from even having the option to do it.   On that topic: If you're decently familiar with D&D, I recommend Honor Mode to get a fair challenge. It's not [b]that[/b] difficult. I spent a lot of time maximizing my party and fiddling with items to get the best possible builds, resulting in me completely destroying the last boss without breaking a sweat. Should you perish on a boss, you don't need to start over from scratch; you get the option of changing your game mode from Honor to Custom, so you don't lose all the progress you've made.  [hr][/hr] If you're curious about my specific build: [spoiler]All characters were single class. My main character was a Halfling Light Cleric, equipped with all items with Radianting Orb.  Karlach was a Fighter / Eldritch Knight with tavern brawler. A throwing build that obliterated enemies.  Minthara was a Fighter / Arcane Archer, focused on status effects. She generously applied Reverberation, Dzed, Prone, Frightened and Blind to our enemies. Did decent damage, but nowhere near what an archer build can output if built around it.  The last varied a bit, but at the final portion of the game I used Lae'Zel as a Lore Bard. She took Crusader's Mantle to constantly buff Karlach and Minthara.   Pro tip: A lot of enemies in the game are programmed to attack the closest target, no matter who they are. The level 6 Bard feature Countercharm produces a summon that can absorb a lot of attacks, that is completely invulnerable to all damage and effects.[/spoiler]

As a lifelong D&D fan and someone who’s been gaming for decades, Baldur’s Gate 3 hits a level of quality and passion that’s honestly rare in modern games. This is the closest any game has come to capturing the feeling of sitting around a table with friends, rolling dice, and watching a story unfold in ways you never expected.    I’ve already put over 400 hours into BG3, and I can easily see myself sinking another 400 without hesitation. Every playthrough feels fresh thanks to the incredible writing, branching choices, and the sheer amount of detail packed into every corner of the world.    The graphics are stunning, the sound design is rich and immersive, and the music is absolutely unforgettable. But what really stands out is the obvious love and effort Larian poured into this game. It’s polished, it’s ambitious, and it respects the player’s time and imagination in a way few games do anymore.    I genuinely wish Larian were making Baldur’s Gate 4, because they’ve proven they understand this universe and its spirit better than anyone. BG3 isn’t just a great game, it’s a masterpiece, and one I’ll be coming back to for years.

For the longest time, Fallout New Vegas was my favorite RPG and I didn't think anything would top that... until I played Baldur's Gate 3. The RPG elements in this game are so deep and unlike any other game that you will not experience every little thing this game has to offer in just one playthrough (I've admittedly only played through the game once but think I will do another run in the future). It is also because of that not every playthrough of the game will be like the last (between the character you choose to play, what build you go with, what dialogue choices you make, etc.) which can keep you coming back! The modding community has also been coming up with various things that will also freshen up the experience so that even if you've played through the vanilla game multiple times, it will give you something new to experience (custom campaigns are also being worked on!!!). As someone who has never played Dungeons and Dragons, this I feel does a good job giving you an experience that is like it which I'm also grateful for. At $60 (as of posting the review), I think the game is worth every single penny, but it can also be had for less when it goes on sale; either way, I can't recommend this game enough!

Baldur's Gate 3 is an excellent CRPG whose biggest faults often come from the D&D license but is greatly enhanced by high production values that allowed for some of the best voice acting and motion capture in the industry, highly reactive quests where even minor quests and character interactions can heavily influence what options and how quests are available to you later on, frequent small world building details, a soundtrack that mixes instrumental with a well used leitmotif and vocal performances, and it has some of the best written companion characters in the genre along with regular NPCs that have memorable personalities and conversations among themselves. Larian's unique mechanics that they have taken from their past titles enhances the limited options that a D&D ruleset would typically make available to you, while the D&D rules and voiced characters give an easier starting point for newer players to get into the genre.  Keeping with the character options in Divinity Original Sin 2, you can make a custom character or play the game as one of the game's main six companions. Playing as one of them will allow for some unique conversation options, interactions that might include unique scenes or abilities you can only get as them, and endings. They each tend to have their own unique elements like Karlach monologuing to herself or Shadowheart having visions of her past or moments where Shar speaks to her that you would otherwise only see as a moment where her scar appears to hurts her. Astarion has moments where he can bite people you meet and different ways to reveal himself as a vampire to companions if he bites them before they know. Some are able to get unique items or cutscenes like Gale with his cat or Astarion meeting his former master in a dream. As all the character join you and you don't end up only being able to take three with the others dying like in Divinity OS2, playing as a companion character seems like a thing best for future playthroughs or for multiplayer as you would be losing the conversations and events you would have had with the character you are now playing as, and even while playing as your own character, you often can see unique conversations and interactions from your other companions by having them interact with certain objects or characters instead of you. You can play as a unique origin character by playing as the Dark Urge, a customizable character that can not remember their past but frequently is filled with murderous feelings and thoughts and seen meets someone who wants them to give into those desires. Listening to or fighting those feelings are unique to that character and new side events that also give you a closer connection to events from the previous Baldur's Gate games and some of this game's villains and side encounters.  Your main companion characters are almost all excellent with two being two of the best in the genre. Astarion has a traumatic backstory that influences everything about him and an actor capable of portraying the character as both a haughty uncaring playful near sociopath one moment to a nearly broken man full of self doubt and no longer knowing how to help or support those around him the next. With both personas frequently at odds with each other in your early conversations, multiple ways for his story to take your relationship, and probably the most varied choices I've seen when it comes to your seemingly unimportant side conversations with some options detailing much more about his personality than you would expect due to a combination of strong writing and acting. Karlach is the other high point when it comes to companions with her own well used story of a very fun characters, perfectly voice acted, who is also someone who has gotten back a life she has wanted while coming to terms with it all likely coming to an end soon. Even with a lot of the main storyline and being infected with mind flayer tadpole that can turn you all into mind flayers not being all that interesting, both Astarion and Karlach are again given a more interesting narrative by being two characters who have reason to possibly want to change into a mind flayer as a desperate means of not returning to their old lives, even your own character has nothing to do with the story unless you pick the Dark Urge background. Apart from just the normal expected interactions while traveling around some have additional story moments with Karlach running into old friends while traveling around or Shadowheart recovering memories from her past when interacting with the environment. Lae'zel frequently has interactions when running into different groups of her race.  The only low point of your main companions comes from Wyll who went through a lot of character changes and an actor change near the end of development and is given next to nothing compared to other characters, even in parts of the game where you would assume he would have a bigger role. Funny enough, even what things are partly given to Wyll are also given to Karlach; the man who is controlling his father is her main enemy, the devil he sold his soul to also knows Karlach, and even one of Karlach's endings can be shared with Wyll and your character where Wyll just stands in the background in the final scene while the two of you talk. Wyll seems to have fewer conversations with other characters and a lot of those are him annoying Shadowheart and Lae'zel by flirting with them. Even in camp some get more details with personal items like Karlach's stuffed teddy bear Clive or her happily dancing, Gale's variety of tools and wizard items, Lae'Zel repairing her weapons on a grindstone or attacking her training dummy, Astarion throwing knifes into the air, Minsc does stretches, shaves his head, and plays a drum, Jaheria has messenger rats visit her, while Wyll seems to just stand around or stand around with a goblet in his hand. Even the late joining NPCs Jaheria and Halsin have some good interactions. When it comes to returning characters from the past games Jaheira is done well, Minsc has a few moments for what little time he has, but none of the character's returning as enemies are handled well. Which is somewhat more a fault of D&D itself than with Larian as the company made some horrible decisions with what was canonical from BG1 and BG2 before this game was made. Minsc may be more cameo than character but he does have the ability to summon Boo as a familiar and various ways to give him a build that can focus almost entirely on throwing Boo at enemies to cause damage and blindness while he yells, "Go for the eyes!" Wyll seems like an afterthought even in the quests he is given some ties to.  Even the minor NPCs tend to get more personality than you would find in many games with even what would typically be "trash mob" encounters with standard enemies typically giving you people you can see some personality from. That either coming from their ambient dialogue or by conversing with them before a fight, or before you avoid it through your conversation options, or by turning some characters to your side, or by using the speak with dead spell to talk to certain dead characters. The world is full of random characters with brief but memorable interactions when engaging them or listening to the conversations they have with one another. A man singing about washing his armpits before noticing you watching him bathe, an overly friendly shopkeeper, a tired clerk who who is in enough trouble already and will just let you steal from the private vaults, a book club meeting, a newly married couple possibly managing to survive multiple threats while making the journey to Baldur's Gate, a tome seller who asks for quiet to not disturb the magical tomes, nearly ever animal you meet and cast speak to animal on, etc.  Full Review: https://backloggd.com/u/Kennan/review/4744451/ https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JImMRcR7OHU

This game deserves all it's praises. For what an RPG is supposed to be, it does everything right and then some.  The strongest point in its favor is the sheer reactivity of the world to your choices not only during the events of the game, but also in character creation. There are so many ways to experience the story of BG3. I feel like I could easily play through another 10 times and still be surprised at what I find.  While the Forgotten Realms' kitchen-sink fantasy isn't exactly my cup of tea, the story focuses much more on the internal struggle of the party rather than the character of the setting, which is fine since it is done very well. I found all of the origin characters' plights moving and grew very fond of them by the ending.  The game's combat mechanics are also top-notch. It can be difficult to retain the feel of a TTRPG while also tweaking the system to work better in a video game format, but as an experienced D&D 5e player, I can affirm that this adaptation is great. Some of the design choices in later boss battles were so creative, that I would even go as far as saying that Larian currently has a better grasp of the 5e system than WotC does at times.  Finishing BG3 took me 127 hours in game and 3 years irl to complete. I need a long break before I'm willing to commit myself to another playthrough, but I can't wait to play it again.

What is there to say that others haven't already. So rather some feedback for people still on the fence.    - Turn-based fighting: yes, it slows down the action BUT in turn you get way more strategic fighting where environment, classes, conditions etc can turn the tide of battle in an instance. It takes about 10-20h play time before getting to real action but soon you'll find yourself revising strategies and leveraging weaknesses for strength.    - Make your own journey: despite many mentioning this, the philosophy of this game is that you play it and have a custom storyline. Leave FOMO behind, you won't/shouldn't do everything in your first run. Rather you should explore wacky characters, hidden dungeons, plot twists and move on when you want to. You can lose hours in this game or keep a good tempo by focussing on the main quests. The choice is yours.    - Balance playing and wiki'ing: it's easy to spend hours on wiki's, reading up on the best gear, ways to defeat boss A or boss B, or the "right" choices to make. While I understand wiki'ing when you're stuck, need a hand or want to understand mechanics, it also kills some of the fun, plots and spontaneity of the game. Trust me when I say that for the majority of quest, there are typically several ways to arrive at the same point, be it sometimes with different results. But the game guides you without feeling too much guidance.    Have fun!

Yes, BALDUR’S GATE 3 is everything they promised and more.  The long, long-distant threequel to Baldur’s Gate and Baldur’s Gate II, a pair of 1990s roleplaying staples, BALDUR’S GATE 3 is a Dungeons & Dragons video game, big brand and all, for the modern age, and it sets the bar as THE defining example of a D&D game. Rolling dice, scoring natural 20s, laying on hands, speaking with animals, picking locks and shapechanging – it’s all here. It’s long to play and thorough to explore, lavishly produced, fully voice-acted, written with sharp wit, sarcastic sneers, and poetic doom – yes, this is exactly the game everyone has been raving to you about. What a rarity that is in this day and age, huh? But if you, like me, are going into BG3 with almost no knowledge of the previous games (maybe intentionally, as those pillars of the genre are something like 200 hours of play), don’t worry. This third title holds up on its own, and mostly separate from the others, with the history explained quite naturally via conversation and books. There IS a great deal of fanservice, but if you aren’t one of those old fans, you probably won’t notice the service.  The adventure hook is this: upon completion of your character sheet, you and your friends (you can choose to play co-op, through the ENTIRE campaign!) are kidnapped by an alien ship and implanted with hideous mindworms that will, in time, turn you into aliens yourself – but you notice that, for some reason, that isn’t happening. And what’s more, these mindworms seem to have given you (and every other one of their victims) psychic powers. So you’re not mutating, but you might at any moment, you’re hearing people’s thoughts, you’re having bizarre nightmares, and every teammate you meet has their own wild backstory besides. Just another day in the old Sword Coast. D&D has never played this smoothly in a computer setting, either – dice rolls are fast, animations are punchy, effects are merciless. You will learn the devastation that an innocuous spell like Grease can have on a party, or how badly you need Sleep to work, right now, or this troll will turn you into a carpet stain. You will see how important it is that a paladin not go down, because no one else on your team can dish out the pain like they can, and no one else can withstand the crackback. Yes, you will polymorph that guy into a sheep.  The defining charm of any computer role-playing game is, of course, the cast of characters, and Larian Studios have done them all up to the nines, some playing to old D&D types, and some against. There’s the Rex-Harrison-esque vampire, inexplicably walking in the sun, there’s the very stereotypical wizard with a Shakespearean tongue and fewer restraints on himself than any of us would like, and there’s the lovable, brick-headed barbarian devil with a heart condition, just to name a few. If you’ve ever played Mass Effect or Dragon Age, those old vibes hit precisely right, as your character slots into this crew of total whackos. You don’t need to like them all, or even speak to them all, but, be prepared for them to not be so kind to you in turn. The villains are the weaker side of the cast, but they’re all fun to hate in their own ways - fun to trick, fun to backstab, fun to curse with uncontrollable dancing. They can even make intriguing allies of circumstance, if you’re willing to play a bit looser with your character’s morals, or if you’re playing your role with a bit more sneering and murder than others might.  BALDUR’S GATE 3 is a very permissive game, and it has an extremely flexible plot – for example, in one of the two playthroughs I’ve had, we intentionally ignored a handful of characters early on, finding them obnoxious or untrustworthy... and they left. I don’t mean they left the party and were instead found elsewhere, I mean they exited the story, entirely! We never saw them again, no quests, no explanations, nothing, and major chunks of the plot were simply siloed off from us. This has real gameplay consequences, too, as suddenly you’re without one of your precious few allies, forever, so everything beyond that point will become much harder. It’s a perfectly natural consequence, and I loved the extra friction, but I have to say, VERY few games have the balls to do that to a player. It definitely colored our adventure differently from then on, but if you feel like it fits your story, then by all means do it. It is a ROLE playing game, after all. Play your role! This game will let you do it, rewarding you with its right hand and punishing you with its left!  That’s not to say the writing is immaculate – a story as massive as this will always have its missteps. For one thing, certain characters have to make critical decisions in their own story arcs, but they will always defer those choices to the player, in a bizarre refusal of agency. For another, every character’s romance options are Yes… or in other words, since they couldn’t design for the myriad sexualities of every single player, the designers instead made all of the main cast “politely bisexual” (to borrow from Noah Gervais). Any given teammate is merely waiting for the player character to hit a certain Relationship Score to flip their Love Boolean to “true,” after which point they will be rapturous romantics, no questions asked – though you can still disappoint them, if you’re not attentive. This works fine enough, if you understand them to be responding to your actions, not your physique, and this is … “better” … than previous games in the genre, which infamously cut girls fewer breaks than boys, or didn’t allow homosexual relationships at all, but … I don’t know. It seems extremely odd for these characters, whose lives are so thoroughly explored otherwise, to be reducible to “buckets filling with points,” who will let the player make critical life choices for them, and who have no true boundaries. It feels cynical, mechanical, old-fashioned, like players should be in total control over their digital dollies, storytelling be damned. I really wish that a better design, for romance especially, would come to this genre at some point, but, that’s how the d20 tumbles.  Nor is this game without its technical hiccups. Character pathing is a real struggle, as computer teammates try and fail to find safe routes to follow the player, and so need to be “unhooked,” nudged in the right direction, and “rehooked” to follow again. In co-op campaigns, every human player is treated as The Protagonist, which causes some odd sentences to come out of characters that one player has met, but another has never seen before. Not every quest has accounted for every possible playthrough, leaving a handful of late-game events to stall out, or dead-end. You are allowed to forbid other players from seeing private moments, but sometimes the game will load parts of those private moments into the world anyway, leaving characters to stand stock-still, mannequin-like, until you are finished. For a game so huge, it’s natural that some clumsiness would snag it, but wow, does it ever snag.  And perhaps the game’s biggest drag is its crushing length, at 90 hours and more – or, depending on how long you’ve been waiting and how much fun you’re having, maybe that’s its biggest upside! In true Smash Ultimate fashion, the developers had no clue if there will ever be a Baldur’s Gate 4 at all, and so crammed every last ounce of game into this game, for you to play and play and play until you have had your fill. So fill up! Even if you aren’t huge on D&D, even if you aren’t huge on computer RPGs, this is one of those titles that comes along once in a decade, sweeps the playing field, and takes every award it rightfully earned. It’s so, so rare for a AAA project, and a revival-reboot at that, to come out as stylish and as huge and as lovingly sculpted as this one has, so let’s enjoy them when they do appear. Let’s roll up a new character and slip into Faerûn, for old time’s sake, and for new times too.

[h1]TL;DR[/h1] [b][u]Baldur’s Gate 3 stands as the pinnacle of modern computer role-playing games, showcasing unparalleled production values and overall excellence.[/u][/b] For many players, it may well represent the finest RPG of the modern era—an extraordinary passion project that is worth every praise.   [h1] Chances are for the prepared[/h1]  [b][u] The title experienced an unexpected surge in pre-release attention[/u][/b]  following an accidental showcase of unconventional content, which highlighted the game’s remarkable degree of player freedom. Audiences were captivated by the sheer breadth of choices seemingly available. [b][u] Upon launch, the game more than fulfilled these expectations[/u][/b] , delivering an astonishing array of fully animated and voice-acted paths that surpassed most predictions. While the accidental publicity undoubtedly amplified its visibility, [b][u] only exceptional quality could have converted that initial curiosity into lasting acclaim and a devoted fanbase.[/u][/b]  It is this intrinsic excellence that continues to sustain the game’s widespread recognition.  [h1]Defining Strengths[/h1]  [h3]Presentation[/h3] [b][u] One of the game’s most impressive achievements lies in its fully voiced and motion-captured dialogue.[/u][/b]  Every conversation is brought to life with professional performances and seamless animations, lending the world a cinematic depth rarely seen in the genre.   [h3]Player Freedom[/h3] Beyond presentation, [b][u] the developers demonstrated an almost obsessive commitment to player agency.[/u][/b]  Countless possibilities were accounted for, allowing the world to respond organically to an extraordinary range of decisions. This dedication to freedom and reactivity demanded immense time and resources, yet the result feels remarkably immersive. It is heartening to see a studio like Larian pursue such an ambitious vision with genuine passion.   [h3]Developer Support[/h3] Equally noteworthy is the studio’s [b][u] unusually generous post-launch support.[/u][/b]  Rather than limiting updates to essential bug fixes, the developers continued to expand the game for well over a year, introducing new voice lines, fully animated cutscenes, and even entirely new classes and abilities. Such sustained investment far exceeds typical expectations and has elevated an already masterful work to even greater heights.  Collectively, these qualities have helped demonstrate that CRPGs—despite their traditionally steep learning curve—can achieve mainstream success and attract newcomers to the genre, thereby reinvigorating interest in turn-based role-playing experiences.  [h1]A Notable Flaw [/h1] Nevertheless, the game is not without constraints. Its adherence to the Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition ruleset, while authentic to the tabletop experience, was never designed with digital implementation in mind. As a result, [b][u] the combat system feels somewhat restrictive compared to Larian’s previous title, Divinity: Original Sin 2,[/u][/b] whose more flexible mechanics offered greater tactical enjoyment.  [h1]Overall[/h1] In essence, Baldur’s Gate 3 is a heartfelt tribute to the CRPG tradition, turn-based combat, and the spirit of open-ended Western role-playing. It is a work of extraordinary passion and craftsmanship—one that sets a new benchmark for the genre. [h2] Baldur's Gate 3 is my GOTY for at least the next decade. [/h2]   This game is truly a diamond in the rough. After 500 hours, no other game has kept me coming back for more again and again, playthrough after playthrough, campaign after campaign. The depth this masterpiece of a game has is unmatched. This is a game with limitless replay value, as your decisions truly matter and will lead to a cavalcade of fun, new experiences, even as I approach hour 600. I recently completed my Honor(less) run (sorry Gale), and it is one of my proudest achievements in gaming.  As someone who has been playing 5th edition Dungeons and Dragons (DND) since its inception in 2014, Larian studios does an excellent job bringing the land of Faerun to life in this adaptation. This game was also my introduction to the CRPG format, a genre which has become a fast favourite of mine. That said, 5th edition DND is not without its flaws and ... [i] quirks [/i], and BG3 brings those over as well. I have since gone back to explore Larian's previous title, Divinity Original Sin 2, and while it does not have the same charm or familiarity that BG3 and DND do for me, I would still wholly recommend any BG3 fan give Divinity a try. Not to glaze Larian too much though, as they have gotten much better praise from people who's opinion matter a lot more than just one guy at his desk.  Finally, most of all, what makes this game special to me is the multiplayer aspect. I as a gamer tend to enjoy more single-player experiences, and often struggle to find games I enjoy with friends vs on my own. This game manages to provide an enthralling experience regardless of player count. It has been a source of great joy for me these past few years being able to connect with friends new and old over Baldies Great 3.  Thank you to everyone who made this wonderful gift of a game, and thank you to you reader for taking the time to read this.

Baldur’s Gate 3 stands out as an RPG where every choice genuinely matters. Conversations, major decisions, friendships, and betrayals all shape the direction of your journey, making each playthrough feel different and meaningful.  What impressed me most is the incredible depth and freedom the game offers. There’s rarely a single “correct” way to handle a situation — you can battle your way through, use persuasion, sneak around obstacles, explore hidden paths, or experiment with completely unconventional ideas. More than once, I was surprised that the game actually supported something I didn’t expect to work.  The world feels alive because it responds to your actions. Companions react to your behavior, past decisions come back later, and even minor choices can create major consequences. That level of interactivity and player agency is what truly makes Baldur’s Gate 3 exceptional.  If you enjoy RPGs that reward creativity, freedom, and meaningful decision-making, Baldur’s Gate 3 is an easy recommendation.

Good, but overrated. I know people talk about this game like it's the second coming of CRPG's, but it's just a solidly good CRPG with some issues, particularly QoL issues and unresolved bugs.  The way the conversations and gameplay cover so many classes, options, skills, and playstyles is genuinely impressive, but that's the only really impressive thing about this game. The rest of it is stock CRPG roll-playing (as opposed to role playing) fare. Most of the game is wandering around looting things, disarming traps, picking locks, and getting in fights. It's more filler than meat.  Plus it's genuinely tiring to play sometimes. I often end up more tired after finishing a session than when I began. It's not a game to play to destress, especially when you don't have a lot of time to play. This is more of a weekend / holiday game, as it takes hours to play, for a surprisingly small amount of fun. Plus, the UI has issues and bugs, particularly if you use a controller primarily. The way stores  / trading work is bad too, as the filtering options aren't great, and there's no way to mark or filter out "junk" items the way there is with almost every other game like this. A lot of items that should stack don't stack either.  Lastly, the game's systems themselves are more fun than the actual content of the game. I haven't gotten to Act 3 yet, but Act 1 is just okay, and Act 2 is a total slog, and really overstays its welcome and is very one-note and kind of boring. If this were compared to a tabletop game, it would be one where the DM railroads you into a "everything is shadowy and undead for 100 hours whether you like it or not". I actually quit my first playthrough due to Act 2, and only came back due to youtube joke videos about the rest of the game, which looked interesting.  I would say get this game on sale, and try it for free if you can from a friend's Steam Share or whatnot. If you like CRPG's you'll probably like this game, but if you don't like CRPG's, I honestly don't think BG3 will change your mind. It's good at what it is (to an extent) but I wouldn't say it's good enough to convert people that normally don't play games like this.  I know I'm recommending this game, but only with caveats. If there was a thumbs sideways option, I'd pick that.

This is my favourite game of all time. I had never played DnD before but was always curious about it, my girlfriend, however, is very into it. We bought this game on release to try "DnD as a video game" and let me tell you, it blew all of my expectations out of the water.  The characters and their individual stories are so enthralling that you'll want to do multiple playthroughs just to see how they react to different choices. The combat is amazing and really gets you thinking strategically, pushing you to try out many different builds and playstyles. The world is beautifully and painstakingly handcrafted and rewards you extremly well for going off the beaten path to explore. There is so much to do in this game i seriosuly doubt you could do it all in your first playthrough without looking up to see where everything is. There are many long term meaninful choices, something you did in act one you will see the results of many hours later in act three  It stays very faithful to the tabletop while also making some welcome QOL additions. such as making potions a bonus action, or slightly reworking some of the less than optimal classes to breathe new life into them and making them genuinely fun to play. The sheer number of choices you have and the ways you can shape how the story unfolds is just incredible. i have replayed this game countless times and can forsee many more to come.  My only issue is that the devs have said they won't be making any DLC which is honestly heartbreaking

There truly is no other game like it. A playthrough can easily last over 100 hours, with every moment of that playthrough feeling fresh and engaging.   There are so many places to explore that even on your 4th playthrough you’ll find a new area you haven’t seen before, possibly even holding hours of content you’ve never experienced yet. Every part of this game tells a story, even the smallest of things you find will have their own small part of the overarching story. Like a random npc you find in the first few hours later becomes part of a large quest 100 hours later, or a book found in a random basement the that only makes sense later on.   In terms of gameplay it really is up to your imagination. You can go in guns blazing into every fight, or you can assassinate enemies 1 by 1, gather them all into one room and blow them up, pick enemies up and toss them into danger. Most fights can even be avoided by just talking your way out of it, you might even end up making friends with them. There are a huge range of spells and weapons and armour, allowing for diverse builds across multiple classes. The game even has a built in mod browser, allowing for even more replayability.  You can’t say you’ve experienced the peak of gaming until you’ve played this, it genuinely is THAT good

I haven't played this exact type of game before, but I really enjoyed this.  Character development, writing and especially the voice acting was great and often made choosing even seemingly mundane dialogue options a test of morality.  I probably spent as long staring at the screen deciding what to do as doing it, in a good way.  It was also neat to see how early decisions played out meaingfully as the game progressed as well.    Turn-based combat with full freedom of movement was a highlight for me.  This made combat tactical using environment and all of the capabilities of each character while I enjoyed my tea.  Class choices while controlling a full party allows for a cohesive team dynamic with each character playing a role.  While some classes and abilities seemed to dominate at certain points in the game, by and large, the balance was good as I leaned into what each class offered the party as their levels progressed.  You really do want a healer in your party though (most likely a tanky cleric), so helpful when scraping through tight encounters.    As a small gripe, while I was generally immersed throughout, in the final act, a couple of unexpected outcomes and tricky quests had me googling.  That said, I recommend if you're feeling like you've blundered through some areas as I did, just keep going for immersions sake.  You'll get to the ending regardless, just along a different path than you intended.    Overall, I put BG3 on top of any other game I've played quite frankly, with enjoyable moments coming far more frequently than any negatives.

I've been trying to give this game a fair chance because I'd heard so many good things about it, but honestly, I just don't think it's that good. To me, it feels like one of those massively hyped games that ended up being far more impressive in reputation than in reality.  The story has been surprisingly difficult to follow and stay invested in, while the mechanics often feel clunky and frustrating. Too many times I've had the game do something I never intended, forcing me to reload a save and replay sections just because the controls or interface got in the way. Instead of feeling smooth and intuitive, it often feels like I'm fighting the game itself.  The characters haven't helped much either. Most of the companions and NPCs are forgettable, and I honestly don't understand the popularity of some of the fan favorites. Astarion feels incredibly overrated and irritating, while Lae'zel spends most of her time coming across as obnoxious and unpleasant. I know a lot of people love these characters, but they simply didn't click with me.  As someone who genuinely loves D&D and runs two games a week, I was expecting something that captured the spirit of the tabletop experience. Instead, this often feels like a game wearing a D&D costume rather than actually embodying what makes D&D special. So many mechanics and design choices just feel off, and the overall experience rarely captures that freedom and excitement that make the tabletop game so enjoyable.  Considering the price, I don't think it's worth paying full price for. If you can pick it up during a major sale, then maybe it's worth giving a shot. Otherwise, I personally wouldn't recommend it at its current cost.

The name is Baldur’s Gate. Show some respect for the name.    The game consists of three acts; the beginning is great, the middle is so-so... the final act and the ending are a total letdown. By the time you reach the third act, if you’ve done the side quests, you’re screwed: you hit the level cap. You end up playing the final 33% of the story without leveling up.    Whether weapons are +1, +2, or +3 doesn’t make much of a difference—they deal the same damage regardless of class or race, which is super ridiculous.    There are simplifications stemming from the 5th edition, like the removal of THAC0 and changes to AC calculations—they’ve simplified it way too much so even kids can play.    This simplification has really taken the fun out of the game: there’s not a single boss you can’t just slice and dice. You can take down a boss with the crappy sword you find at the start of the game? In BG and BG2, you’d have to agonize over “what the hell isn’t immune to this? Which item should we use to deal damage?”    Speaking of bosses, there were only two (2) dragon fights in the entire game.    One of these dragons is an undead dragon and the other is a red dragon—you can even cut them down with a plain old knife. Damn it, Larian, what a waste...    Under the guise of LGBT inclusivity, everyone can romance everyone else. This could have stayed an option, but it’s gone way beyond that. All your party members (yes, everyone—men, women, gay—everyone) are hitting on you at every rest stop, for ♥♥♥♥’s sake. Even the boss you defeat toward the end of the game (the Emperor) wants to have sex with you...
Clunky controls. Woke themes. What's not to love? Ah well. Another dead franchise.

[b]If you are stuck in puberty, don't really care to question what’s happening on your screen, consider Dragon Age: Inquisition or The Veilguard to be your favorite games, and believe that a turn-based RPG is all about[/b]:    - customizing your character model for a ton of screenshots;  - a dating simulator;  - NPCs who look like Instagram models;  - the need to satisfy various sexual deviations;  - companions who have no agency of their own and dump every key decision on you;  - the illusion of choice;  - massive locations that are often a pain to backtrack through;  - flashiness at the expense of quality;  -most of the game looking like a cosplayer meetup at a fan con...  ...then this game is definitely for you.    Personally, after playing DA:O, ME2, and D:OS2, I found this a chore to get through, especially toward the end. I believe a player shouldn't go into a game like it's a second job, nor should they feel a sense of relief during the final cutscene just because they won't ever have to boot it up again.    The three-act structure here doesn't work the way it should. While the first act manages to do its job, the content in the second act is spread way too thin across a massive map, and by the third act, the narrative and logic around you completely fall apart. Gameplay-wise, the game fails to deliver the kind of freedom that D:OS2 provides.

**Overrated as f****    Let me get this straight. This is definitely not a game for a broad audience. This game is for D&D lovers. If you've never played D&D, just skip it. Now, let's go through some specific points.    **1. The game is slow**    And when I say it's slow, I'm not talking about the kind of slow that builds atmosphere, like in *Red Dead Redemption 2*, where you're riding your horse and actually feel like a real cowboy. No. *Baldur's Gate 3* is slow in a negative sense.    Every time you fight, you have to move from point A to point B, then wait while all the AI on the map does the same thing, and then you press the "Attack" button only for your opponent to dodge. These fights are long, and they're not interesting at all.    When you finish a fight, you start looting bodies and containers, and believe me, you'll spend HOURS of your playthrough managing your inventory. In most RPGs, you often find valuable loot that helps your progression, but in BG3 you can spend hours without finding a single useful weapon for your main character (or you find the same weapon with +1 damage).    Progression and combat are not rewarding at all.    But the biggest source of "slowness" is the D&D dice system. Believe me, you'll reload your save file a lot. Like... A LOT. You'll spend hours staring at loading screens.    This brings me to my second point. Also, whenever you want to do anything in combat, you need to press a button and then click on a target. Sometimes I click a skill, miss the target by a little bit, and hit empty space instead. That can literally cost me the fight. Guess what you have to do then? Reload your save.    The game has a lot of randomness because of the D&D mechanics. You have 10 Intelligence, need to pass an Intelligence check, and the required roll is 12. You roll a 1. Nice. Guess what? Reload your save. And you do it hundreds, if not thousands, of times.    **2. The game is overwhelming**    You don't have one character who progresses through the game and upgrades their gear. You have an entire party.    Unlike *Clair Obscur*, for example, where you start with one character and gradually recruit others, BG3 fills your four party slots very quickly.    And if you have at least one mage or cleric—good luck. These classes have tons of skills. You need to spend so much time reading and remembering everything that it starts to feel like a second job.    You also have four separate inventories to manage. Inventory management is a huge pain. You find an item, read its description, transfer it to the right character, equip it, and then sometimes realize you can't even use it.    **3. The writing and style are just mid**    I'm talking about two things here: the writing and the world design.    I'm not talking about the story because I dropped the game during Act 1, so I can't really judge it. But from what I saw, it felt like very basic fantasy without anything special. The dialogue is on the same level as Marvel movies—simple writing with occasional jokes.    As for the style, the game doesn't really have one. If you look at *Disco Elysium*, for example, you can immediately tell there's nothing else quite like it. If you look at other top-tier fantasy franchises, they usually have a unique visual identity and a distinctive world.    *Baldur's Gate 3* just feels generic to me.    I don't have much else to say about that.

Title: A Masterpiece… but Missing One Simple Freedom    Baldur’s Gate 3 is easily one of the best RPGs ever made.  The world is rich, the characters are unforgettable, and the amount of freedom in quests and combat is incredible. Every fight feels tactical, every choice matters, and the replay value is huge. It’s a game that pulls you in and refuses to let go.    But there is one major limitation that holds it back for players like me:  you cannot freely customise your own full party.    For a game that celebrates freedom, it’s surprising that creating a full custom team is restricted. Many players enjoy building their own party of heroes — not because we dislike the companions, but because we love tactical synergy, role‑playing, and experimenting with different builds. Even creating just three custom characters makes the game harder, because the difficulty doesn’t scale down.    It feels like the game pushes you toward using Origin companions, even when you want to enjoy the story your own way.    Still, despite this limitation, BG3 remains an amazing experience.  The writing, combat, music, and world-building are top-tier.  I just hope that one day, Larian gives players the option to create a full custom party without workarounds.    A game this good deserves that extra freedom.

I can only recommend this game if you truly hate yourself.  For what it's worth I really enjoyed the game mostly, as my hours should give away. The interactions, characters and all (even if somewhat a little corny or stiff sometimes), are crafted well.  But you have to be a masochist to finish it, as you crawl back after every crash, getting more lashes, and begging the game engine to survive a single playthrough, because this game will raw dog you with corrupted files over and over and over.  In my 54 hour playthrough, I've had the pleasure of receiving 3 black screens as my files repeatedly got corrupted. But why stop there, it also crashed my computer twice out of generosity, specifically on act III. The developers must have smoked one to come up with a spaghetti engine.  Starting this game up is like russian roulette, your game will run for countless sessions but it's inevitable that it will give up and die on you, taking your game files and hours of save files to the void. Not to mention if your session does work, you have the delightful chance of experiencing the game as a deaf person; having all your game audio absent, or as a confused demented person; having quest markers pointing you off the cliff or to a phantom character outside unreachable boundaries of the world itself, making numerous quests tedious. I am truly convinced this game allows for multiple ways to interact with quests, or skip them, is because one way or another, a part of the quest will certainly break.  Before you even consider dropping 60,- (or whatever your currency is) for this meal filled with broken glass shards, these issues are not exclusive to me, but for countless of consumers who bought this between 2023 and the present. So if you can accept your fate before starting your playthrough, that Krampus will snatch your files from you unannounced, at any given moment in time, then you'll be alright. Again, I was going to give it a "meh", but after struggling with its problems for hours, I'm going to outright downgrade it.  It's not a good game.  And it absolutely IS NOT one of the greatest games ever made.  Probably isn't even in the top 10 of "D&D" games ever made.  Problems include:  *RNG Bullshi...  At a certain point it no longer becomes fun to watch your characters fail to roll above a 4.  As someone who has done alpha and beta testing on games before, I can tell you that there is something wrong with their dice rolling system.  *Camera Controls.  The way you can move and control the camera is janky as hell, and absolutely frustrating.  *Interface. It's just poorly designed.  If I'm using an item from someone's inventory, if I don't have the right character selected, it uses that item for a different character.  Absolutely asinine.  *D&D Rules.  Nevermind how it's not working how it's supposed to...  It's not working how it says it's supposed to.  For example, I've watched Karlach drop out of a rage and lose it MULTIPLE TIMES despite being attacked.  *Pathfinding.  I've never seen such poor pathfinding on a modern game.  Couple this with poor camera controls, and I've often sent people way off path because I clicked on invisible terrain that the camera couldn't see.  Which leads me to the next problem...  *Lack of a pause button.  Some of these problems could be tolerable if there was a pause button.  If I was able to stop a character running off into misfortune because of the bad interface and camera, it wouldn't be as infuriating.     I'm off to investigate if there are any mods that fix the broken RNG of this game.  If there are, I'll try again.  But I'd rather catch a testicle in a zipper than watch the game pathologically keep my rolls under 5.

Not in this game.  It tells you , you need a 5 to hit and you roll a 13, but guess what.  Even though you roll a 13, 8 points  above  the so called needed roll you miss. I've dm'd hundreds of  games and  played  in thousands.   Been at this for  over 40 years.  Started with 1st edition, and I've never seen anything this  bad before.  Eight misses in a row at an 86% chance to hit.  20 misses in a row at 65% chance, and it gets worse.  I'm having to take a long break from this as  the cheating is just too much for me.  I've seen my share of bad luck streaks but this takes the cake.  If they want to do it right than just tell us the actual number we need to hit then all of this griping from the players would go away.  Don't use lying  percentages just correct numbers, like on saving throws.  I've read way too many complaints on the so called percentage chance to  hit which is never accurate. I woke up after 50 hours jumping like a frog on some platforms like a turn-based Mario.    There are no tactics. There's a mnemonization of spells, items, and stats. Instead of thinking about which hill to place your shooter on, you wonder how the Magic Brooch of Ass you're wearing affects your rolls against the Sudden ♥♥♥♥ Spell.    They advertise that you can fight with the environment. Okay, you set something on fire, the enemy burns. It took a staggering 5% of their HP. So, back to the Sudden ♥♥♥♥ Spell stats. The only effective way to play the environment is to push them into a cliff, but the developers designed a loot loss to prevent anyone from playing like that. But if they stuck to "realism," the loot from a burned fireball victim would be unusable too.    The characters are crap. Protagonists and antagonists. Most attack both melee and ranged at the same time, making tactics pointless. They're jumping around like monkeys (a turn-based Mario), making it impossible to create any sort of structured gameplay. Battles are chaotic in the worst sense of the word.    The monkeys jump around like in a zoo, but at the same time, there are strict rules for blocking characters. You can't send your tank to the front because your team is blocking it. And it's set up the way it is because scripted dialogue set you up at the beginning of the battle. So, good luck with a tank at the back. This happens many times; you'd have to trigger conversations with the tank to keep it in the front. But why would you choose someone else as your main character if a tank with low charisma is supposed to talk?    Therefore, to do ANYTHING in this game, you need to have online help open because nothing is intuitive. There are difficult games, very difficult games, but intuitive. Where losing is fun. Here, you have great sessions with Gemini and discussions on how to prevent Gale from dying in the first turn. In BG3, losing is frustrating because you have no control over your initial setup, every enemy has a ranged attack, and everyone can jump like a monkey. It ends with you being surrounded and pelting your team with everything they can: arrows, spells, bottles of various substances.    That's what the Viet Cong did to every commando unit they spotted in the jungle in Vietnam. But those commandos were hiding, and my team of clowns has to engage in open battle. Often after swaggering dialogues.    As for the lesser sins, the inventory menu is clunky. Like something from 2000.  The camping system is tedious. Halfway through a dungeon, you have to return to camp because your spells and special moves have run out. Furthermore, camping is clearly at odds with the main plot, which counts down to a specific event in single days. Your camping days are unrelated to this; the dialogues constantly state you have a few days left until the event, but you've already camped a dozen times.    If you grit your teeth and endure what I've described, the game is beautiful. Maybe you're into D&D stats, then this is the game for you. But I had to tell the truth about the gameplay because it was touted as a game with good tactical combat, which it isn't.
For me, D:OS2 was a way better game.

This game is overrated due to the branding of Baldurs Gate" and its close match to D&D mechanics.  Combat is over 50% of this entire game and it feels bland (dialog is the another 40% and movement is the remaining 10%). 1 Action per turn, no cover system, missing 9/10 times when hit chance is 70%, too many gimicks to try to gain a single extra action one turn that just seem like moving tokens around from one panel to another in some cases, you don't even get this option for every class and not until you're a good clip into the game. As a Wizard you get a ton of spells and cantrips but most are pointless to use, you just gravitate to using the same 1 or 2 all the time. I found myself heavily relying on Magic Missile just to ensure that I was actually landing some hits since you miss like your blind most of the time even at point blank range. Cantrips are a last ditch effort when you have no more spell slots but are very weak and never seem to improve in damage as the game progresses. As a Barbarian you just have the option to hack and slash and you whiff a lot so combat really has minimal choice required. As a barbarian it's walk up to something and keep swinging one (miss). action (critical miss). per (miss). turn (15 dmg). at (miss). a (critical miss). time (critical miss).  Yes, they have a "Karmic Dice" feature to help with your attack rolls but this spills over to checks outside of combat too and swings both ways. The result is you make too many rolls you should fail (20% chance of success) and fail too many rolls you should make (75% chance of success). Fortunately this feature can be disabled.  Several mechanics were stapled on and feel hollow and pointless: Camp supplies - Types of supplies you use don't matter for effect. Not like you gain extra bonus if using different types of supplies (there's like 20 different items that are "supplies" but there is no point). As long as you have 40 supply vlaue you can recover in full. Not even finished with act 1 and I've got 1200 supplies and I've been full resting after every battle. What's the point of even having them in the game as a requirement to rest? Who is running out of these things?  Alchemy - Everything is so finite you get very few materials to craft anything. And seems like pretty much everything you can craft are things you can find and buy elsewhere. No limited unique super power craftables looks like. A huge missed opportunity here to have an alchemy skill that allows you to identify rare components to use for unique things. I just don't see the point - you collect a bunch of random bibs and bobs and eventually after a lot of gathering you can craft a basic heal potion.... OoooOOOoo not like they're 15 gold at any trader infinitely.  Relationships - Everyone seems super sex depraved. You can bone anyone in camp with a few dialog selections seems like. You have this whole "party member approves/disapproves" to build some liking between characters but it just seems the only end goal is to bone like we're playing Leisure Suit Larry.  Leveling - Leveling is a joke. While you have a TON of options at initial character creation, you don't really get much choice at each level (the few level ups that there are. 30 hours in and just about level 5). You don't get stat points to add in. It just sort of automatically raises various stats and you get maybe 1 or 2 choices on your level up. You get a new spell slot so you can pick which one from a static selection that you saw last level up. Or some small perk choice. There's really not a lot you can pick and you don't really know what you're getting as a choice on the level up ahead of time. It's not like you're getting stat points and a perk that you can count on each time to develop your character. It's like maybe you get to chose another cantrip or spell. Maybe you can swap a spell out..etc. Just very bland, no choice of progression. If you want to multi-class it's a bit tedious and puts you at a serious disadvantage because you'll be getting level 1 stuff for that other class rather then whatever the higher level stuff you would be getting for your class...etc.    There's a lot more issues with this game that I won't even go into detail here (loot, camera, save scumming, critical success/failure, mind-worm skill tree...etc) that I think people overlook with rose tinted goggles of the Baldurs Gate series or being a D&D fan. While the graphics are good, the game is pretty well programmed. Story seems decent - though a little ostentatious with some of the side stories of characters. But... the combat is pretty lame. Yes, maybe it's very close to D&D and when you're dealing with pen and paper that may be fine but in a computer game it feels pretty limited and dull. Compared to other titles that are tactical combat RPG style such as a Divinity 2 or Wasteland 3, this combat feels like a massive step backwards for the genre. I don't care that I can stack crates and jump on them mid-battle when I can't even take cover behind one. I would say people should try either of those 2 games over BG3 if they haven't played them yet.  If you're huge fan of D&D or Baldurs Gate for the sake of the name "Baldurs Gate". You'll probably love this game. But if you're coming from playing something like Divinity 2 hoping for something even better - this isn't it. Definitely do your research and watch videos specifically showing the combat in this game and if it's something you're going to enjoy for over 100 hours. Because that is the vast majority of what you'll be doing in this game.  Update: Played another 16 hours hoping it might improve. It doesn't. There's just so many things that are more of an annoyance than fun. This game is WAY over rated by fan boys. Controls are not intuitive, too much BS for the sake of BS with all the little systems to try to jazz up the sad combat/action system. Pathfinding is god awful. "Jump over the lava to this platform", "oh you mean run through the lava 75% of the way and jump the last 2 pixels of distance, draining 99% of all your HP?".  You just feel like a weakling peasant through this whole thing. 40 hours in, still using what amounts to fallen sticks with doo-doo on them as weapons rather than anything decent. Glad my super awesome armor is just base protection and gives a +1 to some random saving throw I have yet to come across. Meanwhile a bee sting is enough to lay me out. Yup, ready to take on the world baby!  So many times you end up doing things you didn't want to do due to janky controls. You want to throw a potion? Well you can't just right click on it in your hot bar you have to pull up your inventory, right click to throw then close the windows down carefully to not click and throw it where you don't want. Left click on a poison potion in your hotbar thinking "this surely will give me a UI to throw it right?" no no... we DRINK the damn thing. That makes perfect sense. I'm sure there's a million cases in the game that you're going to be GLAD you have the freedom to drink poison right from the hotbar with 1 click rather than throw it. People think it's amazing that you can dive head first into lava and cast buff spells and heals on the enemy and other crap you would really NEVER want to do. It's not freedom, it's frustration and lazy programming expecting people to have to handle this stuff. And this idea of trying to sneak and steal is pathetic. What's the point of stealth and sneak at all? So many things are red and you're not allowed to take them technically because it's stealing but you can. But even if you're hidden and no one sees you take it, magically everyone in the place KNOWS you took it and the entire town is willing to go to war with your over a stolen heal potion. Like W.T.F?  Out of all turn based action RPG's like this I've played this is definitely the worst one. Don't fall into the hype of getting this. I think this is too highly rated by people that like that you can see pee pee's and tah tah's.

Wow...where do I even begin?  I want to start by saying that I have nothing but love and respect for Larian.  Divinity: Original Sin 2 is one of my favorite video games of all time, easily top 10 and maybe even top 5, and every time traces of that game shine through in BG3, a genuine smile appears on my face.  Then a moment later I get reminded that this is a D&D game, based on D&D rules, and the smile vanishes, because I realize I could be playing DOSE2 for the 30th time instead and having much, much more fun.  Baldur's Gate 3 sucks, but not because BG3 itself sucks.  Rather, it sucks because only getting to cast 5 fireballs per long rest sucks.  It sucks because having 28 STR and still failing a STR check in act 1 on normal difficulty because the dice decided to f**k you sucks.  It sucks because utility spells like teleports still consume limited per-rest slots even when you cast them outside combat, which limits exploration hard.  BG3 sucks because it's a D&D game with D&D rules, and D&D rules absolutely, completely, and totally suck.  They suck all the fun out of what could have been, and should have been, the greatest game ever made.  And I know this because DOSE2 shows what happens when Larian doesn't have to stick to a D&D rulebook.  DOSE2 is an unequivocally better video game.  In DOSE2, cooldowns are turn-based, which translates to 1 second per turn outside combat.  Thus, when you want to teleport outside combat, then do it again, you just have to wait 8 seconds.  This is long enough to get caught by patrolling enemies, if you plan poorly or just get unlucky, but you can still do it.  Character stats are also made in a way that actually makes sense - if your character has 28 STR and the check only requires 20, you pass the check, period, full stop.  You only have a dice roll when you don't surpass the threshold - so if the check requires 30 STR, THEN you have a chance of failure, but EVEN THEN that chance is based on the difference between your stat and the check.  So in this example, your 28 STR would still give you a 93% chance of success.  In BG3, your odds would either be completely random or a 100% chance of failure, but in DOSE2, you can spend stat points such that even if you can't guarantee success, you can directly dictate what your odds are.  I know a lot of people have said "trust the dice."  Well, I don't.  I tried to.  I've completed Act 1 of this game 3 times now, including every single side quest.  And no less than seven times, my character with 30 in every core stat and advantage in several areas still somehow managed to fail a dice roll.  I'm sorry, but I cheated myself a god character and the game STILL found a way to f**k me over.  Any ruleset that allows a character with maximum stats to fail ANYTHING is an inherently flawed ruleset, whether I trust it or not.  So I'm done with this thing.  I don't regret buying it and won't ask for a refund, but only because I love Larian and I am confident that the next Divinity game will be absolutely, positively amazing.  But I will never play this again.  Never.  Why would I, when I could just play DOSE2 again and have a better experience all around?

I understand why a lot of people love Baldur’s Gate 3. The writing, voice acting, world reactivity, companion interactions, and freedom of choice are impressive. There is clearly a massive amount of work in this game.  That said, I do not enjoy playing it.  The game feels painfully slow. Movement feels like power walking everywhere, combat encounters take way too long, and the pacing constantly drags. I like turn-based games and JRPGs, so the issue is not simply that it is turn-based. The issue is that this feels more like a slow tabletop rules simulator than a smooth video game.  The dice rolls are also constantly in your face. Everything feels like a roll: dialogue, combat, perception, traps, attacks, checks, saving throws, and more. I understand that this is based on Dungeons & Dragons, but as a video game experience, it makes too many moments feel like RNG rather than satisfying player skill. In JRPGs, RNG exists in the background. Here, the game keeps shoving it directly in front of you.  The gear and stat systems are also not explained clearly enough. It is often hard to tell whether one weapon or armor piece is actually better than another because the bonuses, conditions, and mechanics are not intuitive. The game gives you a lot of options, but not enough clarity.  For hardcore D&D fans or players who love slow, tactical CRPGs, this may be a masterpiece. For me, it feels clunky, overcomplicated, slow, and frustrating. I respect the ambition, but (well I realize I am in the minority here,) I do not think it is fun to actually play.  EDIT: Steam currently shows that I have only played this game for around three hours, but that is not accurate. I usually play offline, and Steam does not appear to be counting all of that time correctly. I have put at least 20+ hours into the game because I genuinely tried to give it a fair chance and understand why so many people love it. This review is not based on a quick first impression.

A "neutral" opinion review A very expansive but underwhelming game, D:OS and D:OS2 are some of my favorite games of all time and I kickstarted them. This game somehow felt like a step back in a lot of regards compared to their previous games.  The scale is bigger and if you're the type of person to find more cinematics=better presentation you'll probably prefer this game as an adventure rpg to the previous ones. In my opinion though, the pacing is horrendous and it's not helped by the fact that I don't think that the story is particularly cohesive or the world more interesting, it is partially mitigated by Larian's efforts to allow the players to tackle it in differents ways.  Add all of that to the objective fact that the combat/customization is a huge downgrade from their previous games and personnally, I couldn't help but be disappointed.  There's not really any memorable area in this game or an encounter that really stood out, might have to do with the game being quite easy coming from D:OS & D:OS2 though.

Where I take issue is after i beat it, I've played through a lot of RPGs and the good ones become part of you.  I leave them with new ideas, themes and characters all gushing around the brain that inform me of new opinions and ideas, its fabulous like finishing a good book; further more I am almost always inclined to start a second playthrough... The ideas and themes are surface level and most of the character and supposed charm in the characters is just them quipping at each other like in a bad film or wailing like shrieking mimsies.
