The game has a lot to offer, but in it's current state, 2 years after release, if you want to roleplay as anything but what the intended path is: don't bother.  For multiple hours I've been trying to tolerate many sequence breaks, buggy triggers and wonky fights, but the breaking point is how the game punished me for having the one race that doesn't let me gain an inspiration event... let me explain:  Spoilers for Act 1: [spoiler]So I'm Dark Urge as a Female Drow, I'm trying to roleplay as an evil character. I'm on the "goblin temple" where Gut is. So I go to the torture chamber to get a Dark Urge inspiration. The goblin tortures see me and I say to step aside and let me handle it. They obey and decide to leave the place... but I have no option to torture the prisoner. I can talk to the prisoner and get information about Halsin and the Nightsong, I can pick up the torture weapons, but I can't attack the prisoner or have a dialogue option that lets me torture him. No option as a drow allows me to torture the prisoner. So the work-around is to use Disguise Self, turn to another race and THEN the option will appear for me to torture... why? Why is this even a thing, considering Drows are basically the more intended-to-be evil aligned races in the entire game and THEY are the ones that miss out on one of the most evil actions on act 1? It's stupid on several ways, and this is a drow-exclusive interaction Larian Studios knowingly programmed without the option to torture.  And if we must point out other issues I have had so far with my playthroughs of this game: NPCs don't react to the bodies of their own kin on ground.... so basically I wanted to slaughter all the druids in the emerald grove, so I, one-by-one, sometimes two at a time, used Performance and Minor Illusion to guide them to some outside area, and repeatedly killed them outside of sight of other druids. And that way I was able to kill all druids with none of them suspecting a thing or reacting to the dead druid bodies.  I accepted to be on a romance with Lae'zel, but with no sex scene yet, it was just the flag to trigger the initial romance. When I went outside of camp, Shadowheart and Laezel were talking about how good we were on bed, even though we never did anything.  Another major issue: at night, Astarion confided to me about his vampiric nature, and I asked about Cazador and his backstory, so on... then, on the same night, the cutscene where Astarion tries to bite me plays out, and my character (and the game) act like this was a huge revelation and they did not know about it. The next morning, the same dialogue options about Cazador and his backstory were there as if I had not triggered them already, so the game got confused with flags.  It also happened that goblins have shot through walls with their arrows to hit my characters in some combat during the blighted village. And it wasn't through a window or crack, it was literal solid wood wall, a goblin was on the other side and Astarion on the other, they shot and hit me, and Astarion couldn't hit back with his bow.  I've also faced many wonky path-finding moments where my characters went through extremely long paths for something that was mere meters away from them, happened at least twice: in the goblin camp and in the dark cave below blighted village.  And there's no search option on the adventurer's chest, which can easily have over 100 items in just a few hours of exploring (which the game heavily encourages)  The game continuously makes several time-restricting scenarios/ideas in the earlygame, which would make you think you shouldn't long rest as that could mean events will happen while you sleep, but the truth is that next to nothing happens if you long rest, in fact you HAVE to if you want to progress character stories. My issue with the long rest system is that it is inversely proportional to the story's narrative and what you would think is going to happen or should happen, and the paranoia that events MIGHT happen if you long rest is completely broken when you realize they won't, such as the hyenas being pregnant with gnolls for several days or karlach being wounded and a few meters aways from her hunters for several long rests, just standing there.[/spoiler]  While I'd love to go on about every inconvenience, some minor and some major, steam has a text limit, so I'll drop here.  So, in summary, the roleplaying game has too many events and scenarios which absolutely break the roleplaying aspect of it, for too many times has my immersion been broken, and basically I can't properly play as an evil character because the game isn't built with that in mind, it's built with you being a helpful hand. I won't recommend you play it if you're as picky as I am. But otherwise it's an okay game to turn your brain off to.

I have tried to enjoy this game, I really have. I've put a fair amount of time into it and just can't find it to be engaging. From the poor companion writing to the dragging combat, the game just doesn't really deliver. Every companion you encounter is just some variety of smug/witty which makes for dull, repetitive dialogue at every turn. Every character being aggressively horny is also a bit annoying and tonally inconsistent, unless you're looking to play a harem game I suppose. Combat is also underwhelming, and frequently clogged with more and more mobs that don't get more difficult, just more numerous and with higher health. This makes combat drag on and on and doesn't demand very much in terms of problem solving. If I had to say anything good about this game, I would point out that the range of classes and races is good, but as far as RPGs go not especially unique. The production value is there and the game looks polished, but there is little to no substance underneath.

Incredible production values, if you want a shiny D&D game, look no further! The game is also mostly good, but I do NOT recommend it.    When combat ends, the game automatically switches from turn-based to real-time. If one of your party members is down you now have as little as 6 seconds (depending on how which death saving throw they're on) to navigate the UI to revert to turn-based and/or navigate to the downed party member before they die.    If you use the controller, tooltips will cover the context menu randomly, but consistently.    The former problem was mentioned to Larian 5 years ago and they couldn't be bothered. The controller issue is well known, but they can't be bothered.    Oh, and there's a launcher for all 3 of your Larian games (skippable with an argument added to the executable) and an optional Larian account to link with, but it's not invasive and isn't a seperate launcher running at all times a la Ubi.    The apparent refusal to fix known bugs coupled with the wonky implementation of D&D death rolls makes me give a soft "recommend against". Oh, and you can only play this game by save-scumming, but that's ok, it decided to delete my last 4 hours of saves and earn itself an uninstall!

Act 1 Druid and Goblin camps are bad areas, If i had not played the areas after I would have deleted the game in the first 4 hours. High miss chances so the fight drags on for no reason. Gameplay rules are poorly telegraphed and explained compared to other CRPGs that I played. Inventory is poorly designed and for some reason trade inventory with merchants has better sorting with tabs,  some special and quest related items are not highlighted as special or items of interest (Frost staff parts, parts needed for forging of special items, quest item in act 2 that removes debuffs related to the area).The game does not inform you that some quests are time sensitive or have time triggers.  The Underdark and Mountain Pass are much better gameplay-wise and the difficulty of the encounters is better paced. Some encounters give you a 'Surprised' debuff even when you ambush the enemy.  Act 2 Gameplay and Story are much better. The area feels like the Asylum from Divinity 2.  Act 3 The game is still good, has some quests that seem like they were cut short or not fully implemented into the game, higher amounts of diversity, has more characters from the previous games.  Sarevok and Viconia are badly implemented; there is no reason why they are even in the game, badly character assassinated. Sarevok feels like he got mind-wiped by the man in black, Viconia could have been replaced by another new character and it would have made no difference. It would have been better if these two characters had only items related to them implemented.  Jaheira had an okay implementation, some parts of the voice acting sounded phoned in. For example, in the first two games she shouts a battle cry “For the Fallen!” and in Baldur's Gate 3 she says it deadpan without emotion as if the voice actor wasn't coached on how to voice it.  With Minsc, they focused on the comedic part of his character. I found his character to be the best implemented. The only problem was his class; he lost his berserk ability and his stats were heavily based on Dex compared to the games before where he was a more STR-based character.  Ending:  The final sections leading to the end boss drag on eternally. There are too many enemies; it feels like they are coming endlessly. If you have a weaker PC like mine, it would be best to take a book and read it while waiting for turns to pass. It got so bad that I forced myself to google a workaround for this part. (There is a shortcut or alternatively you can become invisible and run through it while avoiding the enemies). The choices before the fight feel poorly implemented, forcing you into a binary decision where you must change a character if you choose option A or kill another if you choose option B.  In the end boss fight I had the impression that I would be able to call in the allies that you collect beforehand, but was sadly disappointed (You can only call them in the preceding section).  You can cheese the boss fight by running directly to the portal and ignoring the enemies by teleporting or becoming invisible. After you start fighting the boss on normal difficulty you can kill him in 3 turns. I only got 2 characters through the portal.  In conclusion, the game was in and of itself enjoyable but was not near the Hype that everybody was showering over it.  I would rate it 6/10.

I love the originals and so this negative review is a close call. First problem, the bugs. Dear god the bugs. I have over a hundred hours in this game, but no more than 14 hours in a playthrough as it will frequently crash to desktop. After much trial and error, I found that if you visit a shop and sell stuff, this will crash your game when you try to save about 30 percent of the time. It has something to do with restocking, a bug they introduced in patch six or something. Thanks for that. If anyone else has this problem, download a mod that allows you to sell stuff into a chest in your inventory, not a trader. And never visit a trader that may die - that too will break your save when the game tries to calculate the loot drop. Which brings me to the next problem, loot. Dear god there is so much of it, and not just the filler crap, but magical stuff too. The game becomes an inventory management system game very quickly. And thus boring too. The sad thing is, it starts very strong - you have little, fights are tough, and it feels like a tight rpg - like colony ship or AOD - tight, strategic, where each thing you pick up can change your playstyle, but that passes too quickly. I have played probably hundreds of rpgs in my time and this has the same problems as divinity original sin 1 and 2, as you might expect, a lack of focus. Some parts are strong, but diluted by the rest. I will try to battle through.

Well, I played it all the way through based on the overwhelming praise, it certainly had its moments, but the bad outweighs the good for me.    The good:  The characters are excellent.  Leveling is a significant event, like it should be in a D&D game.  Good boss fights.    The bad:  Fiddly goofy game engine STILL riddled with bugs.  The game can't even tell you if you will be able to hit a character after moving, it just lies.  Goofy animations in cutscenes and awkward character movement spoils many of the moments.  Had to install a mod to double walking speed to make the pace of the game tolerable. I think without it I would have stopped playing after 6 hours.  Combat is extremely and unnecessarily slow.  Just an absolute metric ton of JUNK items to sort through, and poor inventory management options.  The camera is absolutely awful, especially when dealing with multiple levels of elevation.  The game is like half focused around jumping puzzles and ordering your party around is like herding cats.  Camera jumping to every character whenever you select them ensures that you can't just move people to certain positions at a location easily.  Yeah, you can ungroup your party to move them independently, then for some reason the game will randomize your party order when you re-group them.  Super awkward interplay between turn-based and live speed, where enemies will wander around in real time and join combat when they stumble on it. Also, your characters that aren't in combat can just wander around freely while combat is paused. Also, they will just randomly join turn order but use up the action they used to enter combat and it will be bugged for a lot of things (like losing extra attacks for fighters).  Interacting with like a dozen traps in a room triggers the slow ass rolling interface for every single one, even if your rogue will auto-succeed with Reliable talent.  Most of the time it won't properly show the item descriptions when I mouse over the item, gotta just wave my mouse around until it eventually starts working again.  Why in the hell can't you tell your party to move to a known location by just clicking on the map???  Skills/spells that just don't work or are useless for the whole game.  The usual problems about expectations of freedom to handle something however you want, and it gets spoiled again and again by cutscenes and scripted events.    Most I could look past, but the worst offenses are all the ways the game is just too slow.

TLDR: it's a chore to play.  To give you an idea of my vintage, my first CRPG was Pool of Radiance in 1988. I love turn based CRPGs, and based on the 700k+ positive reviews, I really thought this would be a slam dunk. Unfortunately it is not. This game is proof that players will overlook any amount of garbage gameplay if the story is good.    Proponents will say "you can do anything!"  What they actually mean is "you have to do ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ everything".    Here's the 100% meta gameplay: before every battle encounter, you have to join your party, hide everyone, unjoin your party, take one member and launch your first attack.  This starts combat and freezes all enemies in place.  Now every member of your party that is not in combat is free to stalk around and get into position in ways that make absolutely no sense.  You *have* to do this because if you do not, your party starts grouped together and you get wiped out with AOE attacks.  Its such a bizarre meta, and this problem is already fixed!  In X-COM, when combat starts, everyone automatically spaces out and finds cover.    But wait, how does that work with scripted dialog sequences?  That's the neat part, it doesn't!  Characters teleport next to you when dialog starts, then they teleport away, back to their original position.  Why bother fixing this obvious oversight when you can just use DM maaaagic!    I have 170 hours in the game, all of them were waiting for the gameplay to not suck.  Most of it was manually moving items around the inventory.  Food goes to camp, gotta pick up every individual armor and weapon and sell it.  Oh, did you swap party members after a fight?  Well that party member's wares are not included when you click "sell wares".  You have to manually swap them back into your party before those wares get sold.    I hope you like having to read every random scrap of paper you find because you are punished if you don't.  How easy would it be to simply auto-read notes when you pick them up?    Speaking of reading, I hope you've been studying DND-ology for 15 years, because you're gonna need it to understand wtf you're doing while leveling.  Every level for every character is just endless reading for a million different skills.  Then you have to do the same thing for armor and weapons because almost every item has a unique effect.  Though honestly, it seems like the meta eventually turns into "big nuke that don't have attack rolls or saving rolls".    It's baffling to me that you can't swap characters in the middle of a conversation.  So if you're accidentally controlling the wrong character when a scripted encounter starts, you're completely ♥♥♥♥♥♥.  Oh, you were leading the party with tact-less Lae'zel?  It's a shame your 22 charisma MC can't take 2 steps forward and join the conversation.  Why?  Because ♥♥♥♥ you, they were busy writing logic gates rather than playtesting.    The whole game is like this.  From start to finish.  Endless frustration and tedium.  Quickloading over and over again for minor petty ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.  If this were a visual novel or a prestige show on HBO, it would be amazing.  But I wanted a fun game and they did not deliver.

I should've written this a long time ago, but oh, well.  I do really think that this game deserved some of its praise back in the day (mostly 'cause of lack of competition, honestly), but finishing it left such a bittersweet taste in my mouth that i can't truly call it a „masterpiece for ages“.  I think i do understand the core vision of developers. Larian tried to ensure that this game will translate pure, chaotic DnD experience: and, I'll be frank, they somewhat did it with the outstanding turn-based combat system and all the possibilities that comes with it. Unfortunately, they failed on the other aspect that makes TTRPG have it's own renown: freedom of choice, being whoever you like to be and the consequences of it.  Storyline, and thus the choices that this game is kindly offering, have been written in such a straightforward and dichotomical way that you are either Mr. Goody-Two-Shoes or a pure offspring of Satan (wink-wink) with no real choices in between. The problem is not with the idea itself, but with it realization: whatever you choose, you'll ride on the same railroad over and over again with no differences that really matters on a global, narrative scale. Moreover, this game simply punishes you for picking granted conventionally evil choices and robs you out of possible content with nothing in return. The only consequences is, sadly, the amount of XP you'll gain and alive characters that you'll meet as the main quest goes on, i'm afraid.   The overall quality of writing dissatisfies as well, granting a generally promising, albeit not without flaws, Act One and delving deeper into mediocrity as the story progresses further in Act Two and Three, with the second being a strange, linear filler and third an unpolished disappointment with big chunks of planned content removed in a hurry.   And the characters? Sadly, with some exceptions they all seem more like humanized, basic fantasy archetypes/alignment charts put on screen rather than living persons with interesting, properly designed backstories and character depth/development (i'm talking about you, Wyll). And if the Act One tries to keep that a little secret, enriching the game with a bunch of popping out of nowhere party banter, interactions, random events and intrigue, well... later all of it slowly starts to disappear as you'll come closer to Baldur's Gate itself.   But does that REALLY matter in the case of enjoying that game? No, i think not. To be honest, I've enjoyed every last bit of it, playing all day and night long in search of fun that this game perfectly delivers. What saddens me the most is the possibilities of what would've been if the game was delayed for a couple of years for extra polishing, and if the cut Early Access content/story hadn't been cut so unwisely.   Alas, it is what it is, and despite the fact that I left a negative review, I do think that's up to you to try BG3 out and decide everything for yourself, although you should severely lower your expectations if you're looking for role-playing experience and an interesting story: you'll have nothing of that. But you'll have Minthara.

The more I play this game, the more I don't like it. For a game so complete, it's missing so much.  There's the small things, like the Dash action being the only action you can't cancel, to having no confirmation on whether or not you want to end your turn (I've accidentally hit "end turn" a lot and f-cked myself over).. and nonsensical errors, like being told "you can't fast travel" when you try to long rest while a party member's dead body is out in the open. (Why can't it just say "you can't rest while a party member's body is in the open?")  Then, there's the continuity issues. At the very first settlement in the game, I talk to a friendly tiefling because a rat led me into a storage room. The tiefling is content with my presence, but another one walks by and sentences me to prison for trespassing!  Another time, I was accused of stealing immediately as I entered the settlement when I did no such thing. I was forced to battle enemies much too strong because of it, and essentially resulted in a forced loss.   Next, if you don't bring some of the game's offerings of party members with you as soon as possible, you will get trampled with way too many enemies and have no chance. That wouldn't be so bad if the options weren't all so boring! None of them are fun or interesting, and some of them are a chore to have around, like Gale.   It's also way too easy to agro people! I have to save every 2 minutes because any random conversation will end up triggering a massive battle with 20 people!   Controls are confusing. If you click on a health potion in your action menu for example, it doesn't give you options for what to do with it. It just drinks it. You have to click 'throw item' first, then select the health potion.. that's backwards. So many times I've wasted health potions, poison, etc. because of this.   Lastly, the battling itself is just so boring. It goes so slowly! Although it moves faster than real D&D, it just feels so flat and uneventful. And get ready for enemies to have 1HP left and every PC you have to miss miss miss miss miss miss. On enemies at the start of the game.  If you're looking for a D&D-like experience, this isn't it. This is so boring compared to D&D. It fundamentally isn't fun.

While playing any of the Infinity/Aurora engine games, did you ever caught yourself thinking:   - Damn, wish this game had platforming!  - Maybe you would like to spend hours opening barrels and crates to find no loot?  - How about spending a LOT of time in inventory management, due to small and generic icons, and a LOT of crappy loot? - Do you want a really clunky UI, that not only doesn't improve on the genre games that preceded this one, but it also makes some things worse? - You think that highlighting interactable items is too easy, and you want to pixel hunt for items?   If you answer yes to any of the questions, maybe this game is for you...  On a more serious note, this is one of those times that I wish I could leave a neutral review, but Steam doesn't have the option. THE GAME ISN'T BAD, but it doesn't feel like a Baldur's Gate game. Heck, sometimes it doesn't even feel like a Forgotten Realms game. The are a lot of generic places, places that doesn't feel like their lore accurate counterparts, etc...IWD for example got a lot of the ambiance right, and more than 20 years later, BG3 failed to stand on it's predecessors shoulders.   There are also other problems, like some minor bugs, but I find baffling that more than 2 and a half years after release, Larian still manages to introduce new, major bugs with new patches, like with the most current one. Other downsides if I'm being nitpicky: No LVL 20 (the cap is at 12), a party of only four characters, lack of nuance with party relationships, very low variety of armor/weapons, mechanics info (like crit range or calculating base dmg+elemental dmg from weapons is missing), some staple items, like the Bag of Holding and the Holy Avenger are completely absent and the loading screen drives me nuts, loading to 100% and then staying at 100% for a long time, to actually load (the game is on a nvme SSD, so I don't think this is a me problem).  As a last complaint, after seeing the longevity that NWN1 and 2 got by having amazing toolkits, you'd think Larian would give us something comparable, maybe even better after two decades, but, no...There's nothing.   On a brighter side, the game looks good, most of the voice acting is decent to really good (and there's a LOT of voiced lines), The music is good when it is present, some of the small tidbits and sidequests are very well written and the game is long (that can be a plus or a minus...) and very replayable.   With all that said, I'd say I've played 3/4 of the game, and it keeps growing on me. I might change the review to positive after I end my first run.

This game is great, but it has been such an incredibly frustrating experience getting to the good parts of it. Baldur's Gate 3 is a buggy unstable mess of a game that reminds me of Skyrim. It lacks quality of life features all over and I find myself managing the tedium of multiple inventories, potions, broken pathfinding, etc more than actually enjoying the game. It also suffers greatly of the same problem most "high immersion" games suffer from: it has quips, reactions and dialogs for so many little things happening but when you find a MASSIVE plottwist about the game's story and suddenly nobody has anything to say or think about that, it completely ruins any of that immersion. It also seems to assume in many places you interact with story elements and NPCs in a certain order, meaning you can easily miss out on story or find someone referencing things you have absolutely zero context for quite easily. The combat is as repetitive and one-dimensional as you can expect from anything trying to be D&D, thankfully there's explorer mode to breeze past all that and get back to the good part: The story, the voice acting and the choices you get to make are all great. At the time of writing I'm somewhere in act 2 and I have to admit it feels a lot like DOS2 with a faerune skin draped over it. But that's not bad because I loved DOS2. It's a shame I might not see the conclusion of that story as a minor patch, over two and a half years after release, has screwed with one of the mods I donwloaded through the integrated mod marketplace and seems to have corrupted the last 4 hours of saves, so I'm going to put this down for a while.

After 232 hours, here is my honest take.    The maps feel small and confined compared to true open-world RPGs like Skyrim or Fallout. Too many invisible walls masquerading as 'strategic terrain.'  There is no real RPG depth. The branching narrative is reactive, sure — but the underlying systems like economy, crafting and faction reputation are basically non-existent.  The dialogue system feels dated, possibly as a nod to Baldur's Gate 1 & 2. Respect the heritage, but someone should have cut 40% of the banter.  The D&D mechanics are faithful to a fault — low hit rates, binary spells and constant save-scumming. This isn't hardcore difficulty, it's just friction.    No clear feedback when actions are blocked in restricted zones. The UI tells you WHAT is blocked but never WHY.    In 2026, with modern AI tools, a solo developer could vibe-code a more polished experience. Just kidding of course but this game deserved better! :(    Not recommended — unless you are 40+ and grew up with Baldur's Gate 1 & 2.

The worldbuilding is great, but the game is bad. Random traps killing your entire party because one of them took a weird path, multiple choices all leading to the same events with no actual change or agency (why pretend your choice matter then?). Choices in dialogues just causing a game over (Why is it even here if you can't take it? Illusion of choice, so it get praised by whoever saw the choice but didn't take it and doesn't know it leads to a game over). Even if your choices matter, they don't feel like they do. Some mechanics are inconsistent, like suicide bomb creatures sometimes exploding, sometimes not when you hit them. The freedom you have is actually very limited.  Unfortunately I forget to save often so a random wipe often means starting a long time ago and having to do a lot of boring things like equiping, buying, selling. That's on me, but it also breaks the rhythm a bit to be forced to save every five minute.  You can tell they loved this game but it would have been better as a book.

Tried to like it, regret picking it up. Combat is an exercise in tedium. Soundtrack isn't good, the map, at least in the first act, is extremely linear and underwhelming.     Obviously a ton of people like this game and the amount of freedom in dialogue it gives to the player is unmatched, but  it's still a video game, and the core gameplay loop is not fun.

Stay on the path...  I was looking for an enjoyable romp through an open D&D world.  This was not it.  This game is a slog on rails along paths with only scripted encounters.    Let's start with the main reason I can't  recommend this game.  It is a game on rails.  There are no random encounters, no open world, just paths.  Lots and lots of paths that make getting around harder than it should be.  It is incredibly boring.  If you traveled a path once there is nothing new, no enemies, no random events, just a slog to and from your destination.  Want to train up your part early by grinding some levels?  Too bad.  Want to challenge yourself in an area with tougher creatures?  Nope.  Follow the path loser.  How is it that the old 16 bit D&D games got this right, but this game fails miserably.  Then there are the choices that impact your party relationships.  You have 2 options, have sex with everyone or treat them like they are cancer.  It is incredibly shallow and kinda feels gross at times.  My favorite quirk of all is when I was walking through a settlement and accidently clicked a mushroom instead of an empty space only to turn the entire settlement aggressive.  That's fun...  I wanted to love this game, but it is just not fun.

Enjoyed this game but I personally have to give it a thumbs down.   To start with the good things: - The combat system is great for a turn based game and it's neat how you can strategize with your teammates to be more effective. There are also many different playstyles, and my preferred "Big Sword" character was fun in combat. - The entire game can be played in Co-op which makes the game for me. - The world is vast and has cool areas to explore - The controller UI in the game works great and I actually preferred playing it this way, as a person that is mainly M+KB  What I didn't like: - The fantasy/DnD theme was way too overdone for me. The "Kith’rak Ghal-Morann of the Astral Sea needs our help in finding his lost twin 30 years ago but he turns out to be Lazathel-Varn High-Commander of Creche Y’llek" type of fantasy names and story just got annoying to me after a certain point. I get that it is DnD based but it is just not for me at all personally. - The over sexualization of everything in this game. Had to turn on the nudity filters just to not cringe to death at the dozens of "haha sexy time" moments in this game. Super annoying - The game overstays its welcome for me personally. I know that most people wouldn't agree with this as it is a big adventure, but doing the same type of battles over and over gets tiring, I supposed I could've changed my play style but it's not my preference.  If you like fantasy with DnD mechanics, I can see this game being a 10/10 for you. Just not for me.

I never played a turn-based game but heard great things about BG3 and decided to give it a try. Guess I was naive about how different this would be to games like Mass Effect, Dragon Age, and Fable III. Even on the easiest mode I really struggled playing. It seems to be geared toward return players. Maybe I missed something - like choosing a certain setting - but there wasn't enough tutorial or guidance in-game for me to get the hang of it and ended up hating it.  Another annoying thing is that you spend a lot of time setting up your character with abilities you want to play but later are forced to play all the other characters in the party during combat. The UI and text boxes in this game, even at the lowest resolution are tiny and unreadable for anyone with less than perfect vision. There is a slider to increase dialog text size, but no UI scaler. Half of my playtime was searching for a mod to fix this, and found none. The internet told me "go buy a controller to use the console UI" or "Try screwing your computer up by changing its native resolution just to play this video game" and I rejected both of those options, especially since they were promised to *maybe* work. This is a basic accessibility feature that has been completely ignored, and I am refunding the game.

Do I love this game? Yes. Do I recommend this game? Hell no.  Take Karlach for example. You meet her and she’s just standing there all sexy, talking in that deep voice, and you’re like “okay cool companion,” but NO. It’s not that simple.  You recruit her. You help her. You fix her engine like some kind of j*b.  And suddenly it’s not about the quest anymore.  It’s about her.  You start picking dialogue options based on what she’d like. Not what’s smart. Not what’s good. What she would approve of. You start waiting after conversations like she might say something else if you just… stay there.  Then it gets weird.  You notice things. The way she heart pulses. The way she licks her lips. The way she says your name so buttery smooth.  You reload saves. Not because you messed up. But because you want to see her react again. Slightly different. Slightly better. Maybe this time she’ll say something more.  Then it gets worse.  You start thinking about her when you’re not even playing. Like what she’d be doing right now. If she’d be a top or bottom. If she’d look at you like you were more than just a player.  You start imagining conversations that never happened. Moments that aren’t in the game. Things she’d say. Things she’d do. Things you’d do to her.  No, it’s fine. It’s normal. Totally normal.  Until it isn’t.  Because now you’re staring at the screen a little too long. You’re leaning in a little closer. You’re saying stuff out loud like “yeah… yeah you would.”  And at some point you realize. You don’t even care about the story anymore.  It’s just her.  Just Karlach.  And the way she moves her body. And the way her chest bounces as she walks. And the way you’d absolutely do her in until her infernal engine combusts.  Like if she was real you would probably never pull out. And honestly? You’d do it again. No hesitation. No regrets. Just you and Karlach and a bottle of "stuff".  Where was I going with this. Oh yeah kicking the squirrel was really funny. What a great game.  10/10.

What an absolute buggy mess. I bought this game after waiting for years to play it in a later state of development and its depressing how buggy the game is. Tons of the bugs are not even visible until you've progressed several hours into the game only to find out 'X' never actually started tracking and you will not be able to do anything with that plotline now without reloading to a save.   This is on top of tons of graphical issues like tooltips and icons going invisible, characters randomly dying from literally nothing, characters falling out of combat and gameplay bugs like floating players all of which only have one solution 'reload the game' or outright close the game and reload.   The kicker though, tons of these bugs have *years* old posts about them, they just have never been addressed by the devs and when I see threads about these 3 year old bugs where the only solution is to rollback to a save from 5 hours earlier, I just cant support the game.   Fun fact too, apparently a few of these bugs like the floating characters even existed in Divinity games according to my friends who played those, so the chance of it actually being fixed seems non existent. A sad excuse of game development for something trying to ride the Baldur's Gate coattails.  Worth adding that you can play through them, just do research before like doing anything and be hypervigilant. I cant get a refund and the bugs arnt bad enough that I will stop, but its a much less enjoyable experience when you have to keep redoing things because of random bugs.

Great story.  Have read FR since the 80s with Salvatore etc.  The game itself should be much better and only fully succeeds because of the voice casting and the already present lore of FR.  Constant trip wires put in place by Larian who let themselves down by trying to be clever showing the combat log being visible for each action.  I scrutinise every single action in the combat log and often find apparent errors, which are clearly meant for players to not notice and take Larian at their word.  Every game has this in the background but to show it openly is where it breaks down how Larian manipulate the mechanics.  Many tooltip differences where such as  Swashy with rakish bonus skill tooltip where I have to pass my own ac to knock the weapon from the enemies hands.  Reverse arcane synergy on myself (tooltip stated this,) where I received disadvantage on all attacks across all my party.  Skills ignored such as athletics not even registering in the combat log but still taking the bonus action.  A lot of this may be down to honour mode as Larian appear to abuse HM to justify changing the mechanics which you cannot see as they do not update any tooltips.   And the dice rolls are rigged to a degree.  Many times I set myself up to be needing 4's and 5's only, yet find I miss almost as often as hitting.  It hovers around 2,3 & 4 which are the most popular of my dice rolls.  But this will suit the narrative as it restricts the player accordingly.  Nobody spends $100m on a game to have it all come down to a 1d20 roll.  And certainly nobody would honestly show this in a combat log for all to see.  I wouldn't do it, you wouldn't do it and the next person wouldn't do it either. The game is a relative breeze, honour mode or not.  Shame Larian feel the need to manipulate the mechanics and the combat log via the rigged die rolls. Should be a 5/5 without question.  Instead a solid 2/5.

I'm a new gamer, only played DOS2 and now BG3, so this is not from a seasoned gamer's perspective. While I have some grasp of turn-based combat, I don't know DnD rules -- but BG3 assumed that I did, and that made it hard to understand many mechanics for which there were no tutorials. I just wanted to jump in and have fun, not spend hours Googling tutorials and guide books or stumbling about clueless. It was not a great introduction to the game, especially since I'd set it to Explorer Mode and was hoping for a beginner-friendly start.    I enjoy it when if I plan my combat well, I get rewarded with a win. With the primarily chance-based combat model of BG3, best-laid plans too frequently meet catastrophic failure. I felt really limited in what I could do with each character per turn, especially i.t.o. crowd control, and there was nothing to be done to mitigate the effects of chance. I ended up doing my best to avoid combat because I just found it so tedious and unrewarding.    Out of combat, everything was also random, and I ended up only taking actions at which my characters had the best chances of succeeding, so I couldn't do the things I actually wanted to do. You don't get to see how high a number you have to roll before you commit to an option, so that was also frustrating. Failing just one of the many random rolls throughout the game often meant ending up in yet another impossible fight or walking into an insta-death trap, meaning I was spending as much time reloading the game as playing.    The high risk low reward setup just had me so on edge the whole time that I didn't want to move further in the game. Uninstall. Not for me.

Regarding prevailing evaluations, I contend that BG3 is an exceptionally well-crafted game, yet ultimately constrained by excessive structural rigidity.    While the game offers substantial exposure to the practical mechanics of DnD, it falls short of delivering the degree of agency typically associated with the genre. Although such restriction may be justified as a compromise to accommodate novice players or to streamline development, the resulting experience is overly prescriptive and limits genuine player autonomy.    The narrative architecture is predominantly linear and unidirectional. Consequently, players are confronted with a dilemma: either compromise immersion—an essential expectation in CRPGs—by exhaustively completing side content despite the urgent narrative premise of a mind flayer parasite, or engage in repeated playthroughs with alternative characters. In the latter case, players are compelled to enact decisions that may contradict prior moral alignments or emotional investments, thereby undermining narrative coherence and experiential continuity.    The core design deficiency lies in the absence of a meaningful post-narrative state. Unlike The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, which permits continued exploration in a decompressed, post-resolution environment, or Red Dead Redemption 2, where narrative succession is achieved through a character such as John Marston, BG3 terminates the player’s progression abruptly upon completion of the main storyline. This design choice is neither sufficiently foreshadowed nor mitigated.    As a result, the relational bonds formed with companions, along with the accumulated value of equipment and progression systems, are rendered functionally obsolete. The player’s sustained investment—both affective and strategic—is effectively nullified at the moment of narrative closure. What remains is a diminished incentive structure, wherein the only recourse is to re-engage with previously unexperienced content through repetition rather than organic continuation.    This structural limitation significantly attenuates the game’s long-term experiential depth and undermines the durability of player engagement.

The more I play this, the more I dislike it.  I don't like the overuse of the pushy, domineering female archetype.  Every woman in this game is an angry, aggressive wannabe alpha male.  At least what I've seen thus far.  Even the freaking Owlbear acts like a man with a woman's voice.  The game keeps giving me 50/50 dice rolls with +1 or -1 here or there, but when they resolve... nothing changes?  I pass, nothing happens.  I fail, nothing happens.  Fights begin with no warning, like the harpy fight.  Then, you begin 100% incapacitated, and get slaughtered.  What's the solution to that event?  Pre-cast silence, which you might not even have when the event comes up.  Dying seems to be a part of the game's intended design.  So I learned to save constantly, because I never knew when an encounter would occur that I'd die in.  So when the encounters eventually occur, I have a fresh save. I'm not surprised.  I hated the Divinity series too, but I bought the unbelievable hype around this game being good.  Huge mistake.  Maybe I'll put some more time on it in the future.  But more than likely I'll just uninstall, perhaps delete from my Steam library, and blacklist anything from Larian from now on.  Their design ethos just isn't me.

Baldur’s Gate 3 shines in its writing and characters. The narrative is well-crafted, and the world feels alive thanks to memorable personalities and engaging dialogue. It’s the kind of game I wanted to love, and the story alone almost carries it. However, the experience is held back by fundamental flaws in its combat and design. Flaws that make it hard to recommend wholeheartedly. [h2] Combat: A Frustrating Power Fantasy [/h2] I don’t oppose turn-based games by default, but BG3’s combat doesn’t deliver the power fantasy it promises. When facing 20 weak enemies in a single room, I should feel like an unstoppable force, clearing them in seconds, not gambling with accuracy rolls and damage dice for foes that should die to a stiff breeze. The game constantly throws overwhelming numbers at you (unless you save-scum, which shouldn’t be a core “feature”), and it feels like lazy design. [b] SPOILERS FOR ACT 3 AND SHADOWHEARTS QUESTLINE IN THE BLOCK BELOW! [/b]  [spoiler] The basement in Act 3 during Shadowheart’s questline was the tipping point for me. I had to cheese my way through with a firewall on the stairs just to get past the relentless enemy spam. It’s not challenging; it’s tedious. [/spoiler] [h2] Design Disconnect: Combat vs. Story [/h2]  It’s clear the writing and combat design happened completely independent of each other. The game pretends to offer meaningful story choices, but in reality, most paths lead to minor dialogue tweaks or game-over screens. I played as a resist Durge sorcerer, expecting surrendering to the urge to open new narrative branches. Instead, I got a few lines of unique dialogue and some superficial changes. This isn’t Fallout: New Vegas, where choices ripple through the world! It’s a puddle compared to an ocean. The disconnect extends to the game’s tone. BG3 forces you into a rigid D&D 5e simulation, complete with RNG and spreadsheet math, but it lacks the camaraderie and flexibility of a real tabletop session. As a DM, I know that level 1 goblins would cower or die in a funny, narratively satisfying way, not force the player to slog through every roll. [h2] Industry Trends and Award Hype [/h2] [b] Not really about the game, but important if you're considering getting the game because of it's critical acclaim. [/b] BG3’s near-perfect scores and GOTY wins say more about the industry than the game itself. It’s not that Alan Wake 2 or other nominees are bad. It’s that awards, like movie Oscars, favor grand, cinematic experiences over smaller, tighter designs. BG3’s 96% “Overwhelmingly Positive” Steam rating is misleading (not really, but let me cook): only around a quarter of players have earned the “All’s Well That Ends Well” achievement (finishing the game).  [h2] The Community and Criticism [/h2] [b] Once again, not really related to the game, but still interesting imo [/b] The most frustrating part isn’t the game, it’s the patronizing fanbase in my opinion. Criticizing BG3’s flaws invites vicious backlash. The fact that there’s a dedicated keybind for loading quicksaves speaks volumes: the developers know players are save-scumming to tolerate the combat. Yet, pointing this out gets you labeled as “not getting it” or worse. (This is not exclusive to BG3, just a flaw with CRPGs in general) I suspect many positive reviews come from players who didn’t finish the game but don’t want to admit it. That may be out of fatigue or fear of being piled on by the community. Or maybe they just played the first act, enjoyed it but never touched the game again, which is probably the best way to experience it. [h1] Final Verdict: A Flawed Gem [/h1] Baldur’s Gate 3 is a game I wanted to love, and its story and characters almost make up for its flaws. But the combat is a slog, the choices are shallow, and the design feels disconnected. It’s a game most players haven’t even finished, yet it sits at near-perfect scores. That’s not a testament to BG3’s quality. It’s a sign of how low the bar has fallen. If you’re here for the story, you’ll find a lot to enjoy. If you want satisfying combat or meaningful choices, you might leave as frustrated as I did. Not Recommended (after one playthrough as a resist Durge sorcerer, reaching Act 3).

After decades of playing role-playing games, BG3 simply doesn't appeal to me. Larian's approach is more akin to a scavenger hunt than role-playing. Many encounters seem to be built around "you should have known" rather than organic progression, and the game penalizes you for not exploring every square inch of every map. Yes, it's well-made and packed with content, but the experience strongly favors min-maxing, guides, and meta-gaming. You'll love it if you like investigating builds and making the most of every interaction. This isn't the kind of classic role-playing game you want if you want to play your character without constantly checking every wiki page and barrel.  REVISION: After putting in more time, I’m changing this to a "Positive" rating, though with heavy caveats.  While I still find Larian’s "scavenger hunt" style of exploration and "meta-knowledge" encounters frustrating, I can't deny the sheer level of detail and craft here. If you are willing to play the game the way it wants to be played, it is a brilliant experience.  I'm still grappling with the idea that it's a universally acclaimed "masterpiece." The game is a brutal, complex CRPG that frequently punishes you for not consulting a guide or, say, a random barrel. However, it is a high-quality product that rewards a specific playstyle. It's a strong game nonetheless.  REVISION 2: After waiting a while and coming back to it, the same feeling remains: a strong start, but a disappointing follow‑through. Act 1 sets expectations the rest of the game never really meets. The pacing drops, the content quality becomes inconsistent, and many quests start feeling like chores compared to the early high‑stakes encounters. It’s worth a try if you’re curious, but overall I’m still not as happy with it as most people seem to be.

I have no idea why there is a trend in games and movies to use modern vernacular in fantasy settings, especially in a role-playing game. Use of modern expletives were jarring and broke immersion each time. Unlike other games in the series, this felt like a modern milieu with a D&D skin.    The story was ok and the character progression felt interesting.  Mechanics seemed balanced and there was plenty to explore. I can tell a lot of work went into it. Divinity: Original Gate 3 is an impressive game in some aspects. The scale, visuals and how some quests have multiple ways to complete them - the amount of work put into it is truly amazing.  But my god is this game boring. I've tried playing it several times and I could not bring myself to playing it longer than the first act. If not for my friends - I would not be able to finish the game. Some say that this is THE best RPG of all times. Well, in last 5-7 years, yeah, maybe. But this speaks of overall RPG games quality of the last years, and does not make BG3 "the best of all times". - The writing in the game is between mediocre and just plain bad, with plenty of stupid motivations and illogical outcomes/dialogues. - I still don't understand what this game has to do with the Baldur's Gate name. It's Divinity Original Sin that just references "Baldur's Gate" quite often. - The worst enemy in the game is the camera itself. One of the worst if not the worst camera I've experienced. - The main story is uninteresting at best. - I like when player is being introduced to a world that lives by itself, having it's own rules, logic, factions etc. In BG3 the world revolves around the player. You're the best, the finest, the strongest, your allies are demigods and EVERYONE wants to f**k you eventually, whether you've romanced the NPC or not. It's like a power fantasy for little kids, not a "real world" that was and will be just fine without the player. - It maybe has something to do with me being old and grumpy, but I hate almost every companion NPC in this game, they are so annoying. - The fact that you hit level cap long before the end of the game is just... why? - Bugs. Well known bugs still, after all this time.  In my opinion the best Larian game is still Divinity: Original Sin 1. Enjoyed it much more than BG3.

So this is it. The Holy Grail of gaming. The Second Coming of RPGs.  Let me tell you about my combat experience. I see an enemy. I have a bow. He’s on a roof. Simple, right? WRONG. The camera said “absolutely not” and locked itself into a position that let me admire some really nice brick textures instead. I died staring at architecture. Immersive.  The turn-based combat is an incredible innovation for people who think chess is too fast-paced. There’s a goblin three inches from my face. I have a sword. I have murder in my heart. But I have zero action points because I had the AUDACITY to walk here. So I just… stand there. Menacingly. The goblin and I share an awkward moment. He’s probably thinking about his family. I’m thinking about uninstalling.  And god forbid you don’t have a PhD in D&D mechanics. Every ability has a description longer than my apartment lease. “Roll a d20, add your modifier, subtract your will to live, consult the moon phase chart…” I just want to hit things. Why is there homework?  Yes, you can do “anything.” You can talk to animals. Betray everyone. Push people into chasms. Very impressive. But between those five minutes of chaos, there are seven hours of moving at the speed of bureaucracy.  This game is overhyped. I genuinely refuse to believe this gameplay resonates with so many people. We’re living in a simulation and this is a glitch in the Matrix.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

This abomination has almost nothing in common with the original Baldur’s Gate titles and should never have been seriously considered for Game of the Year.    Black Isle Studios was an American company that understood what made Baldur’s Gate special. This modern entry comes out of Belgium, and it shows. It feels completely disconnected from the traditional spirit and tone of the series.    The deliberately debilitating camera limitations are a perfect example. You can’t even properly look up. In a modern RPG? Seriously? It feels less like a design decision and more like an artificial restriction that constantly reminds you that you’re fighting the interface instead of exploring the world.    Item and spell descriptions are vague and poorly explained, forcing players into endless trial-and-error loops. Instead of the satisfaction of figuring something out, you’re left repeating the same failures simply because the game couldn’t be bothered to communicate clearly.    And then there’s the lore. Goblins speaking perfect English? Really? Do the developers even know what a goblin is supposed to be in the Baldur’s Gate universe? The world feels like it was stitched together by people who skimmed a wiki instead of actually understanding the setting.    Where are the Gibberlings? Where’s the mad necromancer and his deranged halfling thief? Xzar and Montaron were iconic companions that gave the original games their strange charm. Instead we get characters that are forgettable at best and bizarre at worst.    And what exactly is a Githyanki doing here? It all feels less like faithful Forgotten Realms world-building and more like creative licensing run amok.    This feels less like Baldur’s Gate and more like some unrelated RPG wearing its name like a costume. It’s the equivalent of releasing a Gothic remake with no Shadowbeasts—technically you can do it, but you’ve completely missed the point.    What a waste of time and money.    Game of the Year?    Yeah… right.

I am a big fan of RPGs; basically every single one of my favorite games is an RPG. So when I heard about a new critically acclaimed RPG that was released, I immediately bought it. In retrospect, that was obviously a bad decision. I am someone who cares first and foremost about gameplay and consistently doesn’t like games whose main selling point is their story. I don’t like the D&D system; I love roleplaying, but the mechanics D&D uses to simulate roleplay and combat make it actively less fun than just roleplaying with no rules at all. If I thought about it for even a moment, I would have realized this wasn’t going to be a game for me. But I didn’t. I had two playthroughs, the first I quit after completing Act 1, and the second I managed to finish the game  On the surface this game has all the things you’d want from an RPG, exploring areas, filling out your map, beating enemies, collecting items, completing quests; lots of little hits of dopamine that make it easy to keep going. But when I really think of the quality of each of these aspects, they aren’t very good. First, and the only one I don’t mind, exploring the world is enjoyable. There are lots of hidden items to find and areas to explore. My only complaint would be that it got a bit overwhelming at times with how much exploring and options I was given. Next, beating enemies, my second least favorite part of the game. As stated, I don’t like the D&D’s combat system. I feel it does a bad job of simulating real time combat and the combat isn’t very fun. BG3 is a video game though, so turn-based combat is normal. However, I personally don’t like the initiative order system; initiative is easily the biggest determiner of how well you’ll do in a fight, so it’s annoying that something that important is random. There’s a reason why most RPGs either have you and the enemy alternating attacks, or have everyone’s attacks refresh on timers, it makes the combat much more strategy based, which is what you want for turn based combat. In general though, BG3’s combat is way too random, with how likely you are to hit and how much damage you do. You could replay the same fight with the same strategy over and over again and get vastly different results. Yes, you do want some randomness in a combat system so that it isn’t a solved game, but BG3 has just way too much randomness for my liking. In general, enemies don’t really have weaknesses or special attacks; most combats feel very similar to each other, boiling every fight down to just draining the collective HP pool, and once you figure out the best ways to do that, you’ve basically trivialized the whole combat system. I have more gripes with the combat system, but I don’t want this review to run too long, just know it is easily the worst RPG combat system I’ve experienced and is not fun. As for my least favorite part about the game, it’s collecting items, which might just be a thing only I care about. Typically, RPGs do one of two things. Either they have a bunch of basic loot scattered about, or have lots of unique loot across the map with in-game ways of helping you find it, both are completely valid ways of doing loot. BG3 on the other hand had a crap ton of unique loot all over the place with no built-in way of helping you find it. I am a loot goblin, and I enjoy picking up everything, but there is no good way of tracking loot you’ve gotten and finding loot that you’ve missed. You either have to heavily explore every area when you enter it or use a guide, otherwise you will miss a lot of stuff and have no way of knowing what you missed. For example, I was doing a thrower build for Karlach, and there was a weapon that I wanted to get. I ended up having to reload a save because the guy who sells that weapon leaves if Shadowheart is your party, which she was. Like, I didn’t even know that guy was a vendor. It’s way too easy to miss loot in this game. Also, I hate how so much of the best loot are from shopkeepers; I want to find the really cool loot, I don’t want to just buy it off some guy. BG3 is clearly made for people to only find some of the loot, which sucks for people who enjoy collecting loot like me. I mean, so many games feature collectathons for a reason. Anywho, BG3’s lack of care for loot goblins like me made exploring feel like a real chore. And finally there are the quests, which I personally wasn’t a big fan of, but I can see why people are. I was never able to get invested in the world, and while I enjoyed the characters well enough, the slight joy I got from these characters was vastly offset by how little I enjoyed the gameplay. Whenever I dislike the gameplay in a game, it makes it hard for me to care about the game’s other aspects, and BG3 is no exception. Although, I do have a couple of actual complaints about the quests. First is that in Act 1 nothing you did really progressed the story at all, heck you can just walk up to the mountains and pretty much skip the whole thing, it made the first few dozens hours of the game feel like it had little momentum. Second is that Act 3 has way too many quests, like I only got through half of Baldur’s Gate and I already have like 20 side quests, some of which would require me to go into the second half of the city and pick up even more side quests, I just got way too overwhelmed. I feel like they could’ve paced the quests better so that you’re finishing some as you get new ones. Overall, I don’t have much to say about the quests, I’ve experienced far better and far worse. And finally, in terms of the story, which everyone seems to adore, I don’t really see what’s so great about it. It’s overall a pretty simple story, with you just travelling from place to place, helping random people, killing bad guys, until you eventually get to the ending. It was a pretty standard RPG story. And in terms of choices you can make, there almost always two options, one good one and one evil one. Sometimes the choices you are given are more morally ambiguous, but for the most part if you are doing a good or evil playthrough, you only really have one choice. And of course, the majority of the game is exploring, fighting, side quests, etc. So even if you did a second playthrough of the game, the vast majority of the game would be identical, with only minor things changing. Of course, this isn’t a problem, most RPGs don’t have branching stories, but with how much emphasis this game puts on roleplaying, I expected a bit more options to make the character feel like my own. So overall, the exploration was enjoyable, but collecting loot wasn’t fun, the combat is boring and has way too much randomness, the quests and characters are alright but nothing to right home about, and the story is pretty standard. As always with games critically acclaimed for their story, I fail to see the hype. 6.5/10

I'm not the biggest fan of super-large-scope, light fantasy stories about the big bad evil getting ready to do some bad evil things, and the good guys fighting against it. I know you can also take different paths and play the villain, but the overall worldbuilding, tone and scope of the story seemed pretty unoriginal and bland to me. Everybody likes talking about the characters but I really didn't find them that deep nor complex. They all fall within pretty banal stereotypes and don't offer a deep level of interaction beside their quests. The story and characters were the sole reason why I kept going, since the combat didn't tickle my brain enough (I DO like turn based combat, but i consider the JRPG type to be more fun), so once that source of stimulus dried out i had to give up. It's not a bad game, just not my cup of tea.

I really wanted to like this game, but after 2 hours I wasn't feeling engaged in the slightest. The main drawback for me was the combat and movement; I've played dnd and other table top games before, and yeah sometimes the dice just hate your guts that day, but *every encounter* I had felt like I was missing 80% shots 80% of the time, while the enemies rarely missed. even with low hit chance.     Moreover, I quickly realized that stat checks aren't pass fail, but rather a random d20+/-skill, which wouldn't be much of a problem except for the fact that your base stat does little to nothing to influence the roll. My roided out tank fails a 5str check because he rolled a 1 and his 15str only gives +3 on rolls. Dumb. Hilarious when you're with your friends and they can roast your luck, but just unfun and frustrating in a game where you can just reload and reroll until you get the outcome you want, which brings me to my biggest complaint...    There are no stakes whatsoever. Unless playing on high difficulty, there is nothing stopping you from saving before an encounter or interaction and then just re-loading until you get the desired outcome, which you will eventually find yourself doing. Even if you want to forbid yourself, you're likely to come across a check that will block you out of entire questlines should you fail, and when you too roll a nat1 on such a roll....well.... yeah    I'm sure plenty of people like this kind of thing, in fact most people seem to. It's just not for me. If you find enjoyment in the randomness, then good for you. I like my games a bit more deterministic

I absolutely cannot recommend this game. Don’t buy into the hype, this is not a great game, and it’s nowhere near the masterpiece people pretend it is.  If you want to play real CRPGs, go play Divinity: Original Sin 2 or Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader. Those games actually give you meaningful freedom, real build variety, and choices that let you shape your own path instead of constantly funneling you into scripted outcomes.  TL;DR:  Dating simulator: 7/10  Graphics: 8/10  Voice acting: 10/10  CRPG: 3/10  Story: 4/10  Gameplay: 5/10  Music: 4/10  Baldur's Gate 3 is basically an F5 / F8 simulator disguised as an RPG. The dice system dictates the outcome of major decisions, and a ridiculous number of those outcomes lead directly to instant failure, unfair fights, or outright game over screens. The game literally rolls dice to decide whether you’re allowed to continue without reloading... that’s not depth, that’s bad design dressed up as innovation.  A huge portion of the so-called “choices” are fake. Pick the wrong dialogue, fail one arbitrary roll, and suddenly you’re trapped in an unwinnable encounter or punished with a game over. That’s not player freedom... that’s trial-and-error with extra steps.  They sold this game as some gigantic RPG with unmatched scope, but that claim falls apart the moment you actually play it. Most quests are effectively mandatory, either because the game artificially locks progression or because stepping outside the intended route gets you instantly destroyed by enemies far above your level. The result is constant paranoia: spam quicksave every few minutes because the game loves punishing exploration with cheap deaths.  And if you want proof of what this game’s audience actually values, just look at Nexus Mods. The most downloaded mods tell the whole story: most are adult mods expanding romance and sex options, and another big chunk are cheats.  A huge part of the praise comes from people who don’t care much about CRPG systems at all, they just wanted a polished fantasy dating simulator with cinematic dialogue and party members to sleep with.

A Legendary Franchise Reduced to Shock Value  Let’s be honest: this game is obsessed with sex to a point that feels embarrassing.  Instead of delivering a mature, lore-driven Dungeons & Dragons epic, it constantly drags the player through intrusive sexual tension, unsolicited advances, and dialogue trees that feel engineered to funnel toward sexual outcomes. You can barely interact with companions without conversations bending toward flirtation or outright propositions. It doesn’t feel natural. It feels compulsive.  And when one of the first defining choices in character creation is genital size, that tells you exactly where the priorities were. In a universe built on decades of rich lore, layered politics, divine conflict, philosophical depth, and complex moral choices, this is what they decided to emphasize? It’s juvenile. It’s unnecessary. It cheapens what should feel epic.  The problem isn’t that sexuality exists. The problem is how intrusive and persistent it is. Dialogues corner you. Emotional beats derail into awkward intimacy. Instead of earning connection through storytelling, the game repeatedly defaults to sexual provocation as if that’s the most compelling form of character writing available.  What makes it worse is the generational disconnect.  I started my Dungeons & Dragons journey at 12 years old with Baldur’s Gate I. That game sparked imagination, strategy, moral dilemmas, and a lifelong appreciation for RPGs. Now my own son is 12 — the same age I was when I fell in love with this universe — and I would genuinely feel uncomfortable introducing him to this as his entry point into role-playing games.  Not because fantasy should be sanitized. But because this feels excessive and unfocused. It feels like shock value over substance. Instead of welcoming a new generation into a vast and intelligent fantasy world, the game risks pushing them away with its constant emphasis on genitals and sexual encounters.  And that’s the real wasted potential. This could have been the bridge between generations of players. Instead, it feels like it slammed the door shut.  To be clear: the technical achievement is undeniable. The combat is deep and satisfying. The production quality is extraordinary. The voice acting and cinematic presentation are top-tier. There are moments of brilliance throughout.  Which only makes it more frustrating.  The talent was there. The budget was there. The lore was there.  Restraint wasn’t.

This is one of those games that makes one realize that less is more. There are so many quests, so many encounters so many characters, an utterly overwhelming amount of content that is easily missable and yet very little of it is actually all that interesting or complete. The story's pacing starts slow before speeding up and slowing down immediately right after. It's combat falls under the same pitfalls as typical Dnd 5e combat, ie. it only becomes more prolonged the higher level you are.  chapters 1 and 2 are fine, good even, I was having fun right up until chapter 3, where a lot of the game really starts to show it's faults. One that is obvious almost immediately is that some companions just straight up lack a lot of meaningful content or development, Wyll and Laezel being where it is most obvious, at least compared to Gale, Shadowheart, or Astarion. This can be noticed as early as chapter one, but it is painfully obvious by the time you reach Baldur's Gate, some characters pretty much have their arc mostly complete and you have to wade through a couple more encounters to actually finish them. They feel like padding more so than organic parts to the it stories. There's also some weird thing with how the game handles events or how characters react that sometimes gives you the impression you unintentionally sequence broke a questline? it's very jarring, it's made me replay entire sections only to be frustrated by the same thing happening.  Lastly, the combat, the main thing that the DnD system cares to have detailed mechanics on, it's as bad as it is in the tabletop. High level encounters are prolonged slogfests where very little meaningfully happens for most of the combat until one side starts losing pawns. One example being the Yurgir fight (it's avoidable, yes, but it is a good example of what's to come) where you take forever to actually get through it, because Yurgir's minions have so much hp, you just spend a good chunk of the time letting your big spells take care of things and hope they get caught long enough in something like Hunger of Hadad to never become an actual problem. So many fights are like this and after a while it just starts being a repetitive rather than interesting set of encounters. The early game doesn't suffer from this as much because hp numbers are normal, but by the time you're around level 7~ you start encountering enemies with 100s of hit points and it just makes battles last forever.   You may disagree, but there's a reason why people who like this game joke about endlessly replaying chapter 1 and 2, chapter 3 is not interesting enough to want to play through and it makes me feel like this game has been a huge waste of time

Beware, this game is nothing like Baldur's Gate, TOSC, Shadows of Amn or Throne of Bhaal. So far it seems like nothing is remotely similar except that the city of Baldur's gate is supposed to feature in it at some point. I'm not trying to say this is a bad game, it is AFAIK keeping quite true to D&D and appears as though it's a very high quality crpg of D&D fifth edition( ...? I think it's the fifth edition, it did say but I wasn't really paying attention).  My problem is that it isn't remotely true to Baldur's Gate games in a gameplay sense. It is turn based, combat is very slow, you have to wait for everyone to take their turns. I don't like this. You have no party formations, although that doesn't actually matter anyway.  You constantly have to roll things and watch a picture of a dice to see if you can: open a chest; persuade a character; scratch your balls etc. etc....  Oh and btw, for some reason Larian have felt the need to include removable underwear and fully rendered genitals. IDK wtf for exactly, maybe at some point in the game you get to roll your luck with someone and have a full cut scene D&D porno, but I digress. Some people really like this sort of thing and it's not for me to disparage them (nor is it my intention, I just find it curious that they spent the time to render all those ♥♥♥♥♥).  I'm not really that far into the game ATM, the story I've come across is well done and well thought out and the 3d world is very impressively detailed, it is somewhat challenged in terms of scale compared to previous games in the series as far as I can see though. Not that some of the previous games didn't squash a bit too much in here and there, but I'm still in the first region you get to and already the scales of time and distance seem horrendously warped.  A character I picked up (easily missed btw) has just said how much they trust me after we've been travelling together for a few days. This occurred the first time I went to camp for a full rest. Which is also weird, that I have a camp set up for a bunch of people to stay at because for some reason nobody can travel in a group larger than four and also, I JUST GOT HERE, when did I go and set up this camp, when did I find this remarkable hideaway, seemingly safe from/for all....?  Honestly I believe it would have been better if they'd kept expanding on Siege of Dragonspears' method: Keep making what people have loved for twenty odd years.

I have given this game so, so many chances. I even convinced myself that I liked it for a time but there are just too many little issues that make this game frustratingly unplayable for me. Controller support is not very good and what's crazy is that it's *really* good in Divinity Original Sin II, so I know Larian can do it. But comparing the controller interface to the keyboard/mouse in this game is just infuriating. When using a keyboard everything is very organized and it's easy to separate different types of spells/powers which can help when deciding what to do on your turn, but on controller you only have a row of radials with no clear organization and minimal indicators as to the cost of using these powers. I would have to spend a lot of time manually organizing my radials into something usable whereas in Divinity, spells are placed on a hotbar where it's easy to arrange them and see exactly what they do. It's possible some of this comes from the unholy matrimony of D&D 5e mechanics and video game design limitations but it's so frustrating that I just play keyboard/mouse. I guess that's fine, but, like, this game is available (and more expensive) on consoles where controller is the only option. Now something that I know is 5e's fault, is that combat is not very fun. It's okay, but Divinity combat is INCREDIBLE to the extend in which I look forward to encounters. In BG3 I dread them, not because they're especially hard (Divinity is MUCH harder) but because they're just boring. On top of that there's very little reason to be careful with resources or spell slots because you can long rest pretty much whenever you want with no consequences. Now, of course, there are little quality of life changes that make BG3 a step above DOSII in some ways, and I'm not going to pretend like the cutscene animations aren't super cool, because they are. This game is beautiful, no doubt about that. But my biggest issue, which makes all the little improvements worthless, is that I just can't play the game. I have spent hours, literally HOURS  just trying to join multiplayer sessions. And even when I can finally get in, the game crashes so often that it feels like there's always an expiration date on any fun I'm having. Not to mention the MASSIVE file size which just feels so ridiculously bloated and makes file verification (which I'm constantly having to run) take forever. What's crazy is that the game was working a few months ago, consistently for the first time since I started playing, and then they released a patch which broke everything all over again???? Why, WHY is this game *almost* incredible but just incapable of fumbling at the last second!? I will probably continue to give this game more chances, because it just occupies that much of my soul, but I do not recommend this game to new players. It's too much of a headache. Also ♥♥♥♥ launchers, bro.
