[b]“I got The Urge… The Urge… to DANCE!!!”[/b] [i]-Creature hit by “Otto’s Irresistible Dance”[/i] [hr][/hr]  I would describe [b]Baldur’s Gate 3[/b] as “bootleg D&D”.  It’s a “popcorn” game, which is to say it’s fun and good but ultimately artistically insignificant, just barely a step above other popular generic fantasy games, and I’m giving it a thumbs down ONLY because I don’t think it deserves the 720.000 “overwhelmingly positive” score. (However, I’ll change it into a thumbs up if the score ever dips below it)  [h1]X-Factor[/h1] Locales are good looking and have variety in them, physical and voice acting are on point, music does the job but sprinkles in some banger tracks, and the changes to the original D&D rules are mostly great and well implemented. Also, it’s offline D&D, so if you ever missed a session or want to see how a build works you can always fire up this game and just do it.  Despite being a series of rigamarole fetch quests, most setpieces are very cool.  Importantly for replayability, even more than multiple classes with multiple abilities granting no end to how you can build your character, is the modding scene, adding just about everything to freshen the game up for a second and third go-around.  [h1]Gameplay[/h1] It’s D&D fifth edition. If you don’t know what that is: You choose how good a character is at something by slotting a certain number of points in six scores that define who you’re playing as. On top of this you choose a “class” or what kind of things you’d want your character to be centered around, each class providing different mechanics to achieve this, both inside and outside combat, along with different equipment to give you that extra edge to succeed. There’s a lot of nuance but that’s pretty much it.  [h1]Story[/h1] You find yourself aboard an alien ship and infected with a brain parasite that threatens to turn you into a monster, so partner up with an unlikely cast of characters in your quest to seek a cure for this shared burden.  A good hook that gives an excuse to be joined by anyone, no matter how good or evil they are, as you need eachother’s expertise to find a solution to this problem.  The story progresses mostly through dialogue, simply walk up to someone to talk and be either given quests or advance them.  The game also features LOTS of side stories and especially questlines for the ones that either want to explore more of this world or to satisfy their inner completionist.  [h1]Downsides[/h1] An interesting and focused story that could’ve been told in a couple of hours was instead overfilled with generic writing and other filler content stretching it to about a 100 hours where the game keeps bumping into weird pacing, losing momentum, reiterating over already-made points, and sending you off onto yet another wild goose chase when it doesn’t want you to get to the point, again, all of this isn’t necessarily a bad thing depending of the level of writing required for your enjoyment.  In D&D players can say and do anything they want whenever they want and the DM reacts to it. In BG3 you’re stunted in your roleplay choices, because of course you can’t have a simple system that accounts for every possibility, but even then, I don’t exactly like how most dialogue choices are: Yes, Yes, Snarky Remark, *attack*. Also, unfortunately outside a few quips almost no companions interact with eachother, only with the situation at hand which makes them feel more like reactionary robots than the alive characters that should’ve been.  From a purely aesthetic aspect I don’t think anyone will argue about how bad the camera system, the overcrowded UI, the million magical effects overlapping, and other constant immersion breaking aspects of it are, although there are community-made mods that thankfully partially fix some of those issues.  It’s very generic. That itself isn’t a bad thing but if you want anything more than what the term “fantasy game” conjures in your head, this is not it. A lot of characters are set up to be interesting in the themes they could explore, but then the writing team ♥♥♥♥♥ the bed and makes them say the same generic trite lines over and over again. [hr][/hr]  It tries to replicate the tabletop RPG feel but fails to capture its spark. What is the true TTRPGs experience? True D&D is a social exercise where you hang out with friends and say dumb stuff and you all laugh at the dumb stuff you say with the plot/campaign only there to give a pretext/context and game rules as a safety net to avoid a guy just going "and then I punch him and he dies and we win". This game is singleplayer at heart while all TTRPGs are to be played with friends. It has a hard focus on story but without having good writing to justify it, nor trying to capture the insanity and/or silliness that is player agency.  An even better description for it would be “Singleplayer MMO from the 2000s” but with better graphics that’s only related to TTRPGs due to its D&D playrules. If that’s sounds like your cup of tea, I’d suggest it, even at full price as there’s a lot of content for those who have the right appetite for it.

[h1]Baldur’s Gate 3 Review – The Horniest Camp in Faerûn[/h1]  Baldur’s Gate 3 is, from a technical standpoint, an achievement. Full performance capture, cinematic dialogue, seamless co-op, and a level of polish most RPGs never reach. It feels like the new benchmark.  And the combat deserves the praise. This is the best turn-based DnD implementation ever made. Encounters reward planning. Builds, rolls, and positioning matter. It finally feels like tabletop logic translated into a digital space without losing depth.  If this review were only about systems, it would end here on a high note.  [h2]The Story That Never Dares[/h2]  The problem is the narrative.  For a game this large and this celebrated, the story plays it remarkably safe. The structure is familiar, the twists are visible from miles away, and the moral conflicts rarely force you into uncomfortable territory. It wants to be epic, but it avoids being unsettling. It gestures toward boldness without fully committing to it.  The villains suffer most from this caution.  [spoiler]Ketheric Thorm is framed as a tragic, god-touched warlord whose presence should reshape the world around him. Instead, much of his impact is delivered through exposition and brooding speeches before culminating in a fairly conventional boss encounter. The supposed avatar of dread ends up feeling like a raid boss with a tragic backstory rather than a transformative force in the narrative.[/spoiler]  The scale is large. The risk is not.  [h2]Companions Built for Applause[/h2]  The companions are memorable, but often in a performative way. They are designed to stand out immediately. Big personalities. Strong aesthetics.  Yet over time, some arcs begin to feel predictable. Shadowheart’s internal conflict repeats familiar beats for long stretches. Astarion’s trauma-laced charm oscillates between vulnerability and theatrical sarcasm in ways that feel engineered for reaction rather than discovery. They are not shallow characters.  But they often feel curated for engagement rather than organically unfolding.  [h2]The Horniest Camp in Faerûn[/h2]  And then we arrive at camp. The emotional center of the story. The place where bonds should deepen naturally over shared struggle.  Instead, it often feels like the Forgotten Realms’ most efficient speed-dating retreat.  The world is on the brink of annihilation. You have a mind flayer tadpole in your skull. Gods are scheming. Empires are shaking. And somehow, within what feels like a long weekend, half your party is ready to discuss eternal devotion.  It stops feeling like chemistry and starts feeling like a triggered dialogue tree. Intimacy accelerates at a pace that undermines tension. Emotional payoff is replaced by availability. The result is tonal whiplash. Apocalypse outside the tent. Romance queue inside it.  [h2]An Ending That Should Have Roared[/h2]  After dozens of hours, the finale arrives with competence but not with weight. It resolves threads, it ties off arcs, but it rarely challenges the player in a way that feels transformative. For a narrative centered around identity, manipulation, and cosmic stakes, the conclusion feels restrained.  [h2]Buyer’s Regret Verdict[/h2]  Baldur’s Gate 3 is mechanically outstanding and technically industry-leading. As a systems-driven RPG, it deserves recognition. As a DnD combat experience, it sets a standard.  But story and companion writing undermine the atmosphere it tries to build. The ambition in presentation is not matched by ambition in narrative risk.  [hr][/hr]  [b]Estimated frustration-free value:[/b] €45  Full-price bucks demand bold storytelling. Here, your bucks are safer on sale.  [hr][/hr]  More controlled spending advice here: [url=https://steamcommunity.com/groups/BuyersRegret]Buyer’s Regret[/url]

Upvote or Downvote can mean either loving or hating a game. Few see anything inbetween.  This game is inbetween for me, but leans negatively.  The positives are the incredible level of visual polish. It's a very polished game. All the technical elements are well done. I'll say that.  The negatives, though, are quite numerous, but they aren't all equally negative. Let's start with the obvious. This game is oozing woke ideology. Even if it won't say it out right, it's there, for all to see. It starts when you create a character, and the gender is separated from the sex. Want to be a woman with a penis? Go right ahead. Nevermind that it makes no sense in the setting. Never mind that it's an example of going out of one's way to cater to a very vocal and insane minority of people at the expense of reason and sanity.   The woke doesn't end there though. Every scene feels like a Disney movie poster. They have the perfect balance of "races". Black dwarves, black elves, black this black that. Does it matter that dwarves evolved to live underground where melanin is closer to a mineral than a pigment catalyst? Not to Larian or WotC. They care more about virtue signaling than they do creating cohesive and meaningful cultures. All they see is skin color. They don't see subtle cultural differences, the tendency of certain races to naturally share traits which make sense to be inherited by their environment and nature. They throw all of that away to make sure that every gender and racial make up of humans is represented in every "race" or species in the game. It's stupid, childish, and down right disturbing.   Because their idea of "diversity" is to throw a paint bucket on one of every character type in the game, it is to be expected that the actual meaningful types of diversity, such as thought, personality, interests, passions etc were ignored. Instead, it's only surface deep. Vapid, vain , and superficial differences are all the wokesters care about because they are what they claim to hate. This leads to very mundane, stale, and underdeveloped characters.  It's so hard to take the setting seriously because of the ridiculous colorswap skin color focused , gender bending characters. They look, feel, and act more like caricatures of real people than real people. Feeling any connection for them is out the window, with the exception of maybe two in the whole game.   Further still, every character is bi, and every character wants to have sex with you after even a single nice word is thrown their way. It's jarring to be talking to a clearly straight character, only to have the narrator suggest we start having sex with eachother in only two dialogue exchanges. This might not seem like a big deal, but it will totally ruin characters for you when they are presented one way, then instantaneously given an abrupt 180 personality shift. Many such instances of this happening soured my opinion of the character to the point I just don't like talking to them. Add in the ridiculous skin color swapping and gender bending, you end up with a cast of characters better suited for the looney troons than a semi-serious RPG.  Moving on from the blatant political bent, the rest of the game suffers from a similar lack of meaningful nuance. Abilities all feel very similarly. Spells more or less offer different colors of the same few effects. Spamming the same few spells over and over, because they're clearly the most effective, gets old pretty fast. Combat in general begins to feel the same after a while. Fights move along methodically and predictably. Occasionally someone will crit, but the fights feel stale and lifeless. Turn based combat is rigid, inflexible, and creates odd situations.  Effects lack conviction. Most everything is just a couple turns in length. The odds of spells being resisted is high. There's not much satisfaction to the spell system at all really.   Overall, the game lacks a feeling of urgency and lethality that would make the moment to moment combat more exhilarating. Instead, Fights move along in predictable fashion, with predictable outcomes, and predictable rewards.   Having to rest every other fight wouldn't be so bad if the player wasn't forced to return to camp, requiring a level load out of their area, where they must then get past a few animations before they can rest. The process to rest adds tedium which quickly gets old, but always forces you to to do it. How great it would be to click a button and instantly just be rested up, with no loss to the challenge or value of the experience. The old BG games worked that way. It was a much better and more streamlined system.  Unlike other great Crpgs, the handholding removes much of the mystery from the game. Virtually every trap is automatically discovered. Their effects are typically minor, and the ability to disarm them is almost guaranteed with a default party. There's hardly anything left to the player's imagination, nor their senses. Hidden switches are easily seen, chests easily opened, most fights easily won. There are few interesting puzzles, though they do exist. The original Baldur's Gate 1 and 2 had far more detailed hidden item searching, where player agency mattered as much as the character. Baldur's Gate 3 does not think highly enough of the player to expect them to overcome adversity, so they give very little of it.   The camera sucks. Controlling the Camera is tedious. Being 3D, it requires constant re-orientation and positioning. Oftentimes it gets stuck on walls, ramps, doorways or any other background asset.  I can't remember the last time I played a CRPG where the Camera fought me as much as the enemies did.   Most CRPGs allow for seamless and fast switching of characters. Due to the turn based nature, each character must be resolved in order. While splitting party members up is possible, it's problematic. Characters can't be group selected, so each character must be controlled individually when group  mode is deactivated. Furthermore, every character has their own inventory, which is completely disorganized. It's just one giant x by y grid of item slots. Trying to find a specific item, on a specific character is downright obnoxious. The low contrast, dark color of the UI does not help with this. I've gone hours without even wanting to go  looking for items. It's easier to simply not care.  Finally, my least favorite mechanic by far is the die rolling system. Ultimately, this is what stalled my playthrough more than anything else. The ridiculousness of forcing me to click on a 20 sided die to resolve virtually every in game challenge is unfathomable. The game will have you rolling a die every minute, on average. Some rooms are so full of traps and treasure, characters will be rolling dice for minutes. First, a screen pops up, interrupting your flow. You then have to determine which modifiers you want applied to the roll. Then you click the die to roll it. Now you wait for the die to finish its flashy roll animation. Once that ends, it starts another animation adding your mondifiers together one by one ( could be 5 or more often). After this ends, it gives the result in a final flashy animation and tells you either you succeeded or failed.... this happens every..single..time... you want to open a locked chest, disarm a trap, convince an npc, pull a stuck lever... etc etc. You'll watch this same animation 1000 times over dozens of hours. This should have, and could have been done behind the scenes without the player even knowing.  BG3,despite all of its polish, has only superficial depth in both the setting and gameplay. They cleaned and polished every speck of character out of whatever this game claims itself to be. Whether its the repetitive combat, or the vain, skin deep woke pandering, BG3 just feels bland. While I don't love it, I also don't feel enough about it to hate it.

This is my first review, and I guess I am going to be the bad guy on this one..I actually cant fathom how this game is overwhelmingly positive. I will admit, the characters have extremely deep lore, the story is alright, and you really feel love for the characters and pretty much get to decide the fate of whoever you want which is cool. However there are so many groundbreaking bugs in this game its insane, On my first playthrough I had to stop and load a game several times and lose tons of progress over the course of the game due to bugs, to the point where I actually didn't finish the game because for no apparent reason, my party didnt come with the emperor during phase 2 of the last fight which took a good 30-45 minutes to go get too, and I had to keep starting over. That to me is just a fun ruiner. When I looked it up its a "known bug" this game has been out for how long and we still don't fix bugs like that? It's a shame... There are also, a ton of annoying spells in the game that are also just fun ruiners, I cant remember how many times I had to restart the second to last fight because of a "snare" where my character couldn't move for 8 turns and just dies, or a spell that makes me drop my weapon and picking it up causes an action so I cant do anything. I don't think at max level something should be able to 0-100 and get 8 turns to attack your character and cause you to reload in one sitting without you being able to out manuever or atleast out play the situation. This game was pretty fun overall, but the fact that the ending was just so annoying and not even fun to play and felt like a chore and how that's suppose to be the climax of the game was a huge turn off. I'd give this game a 7/10 in my book and defiantly not worth all the awards for how buggy it is.

So you have to play one of these bad npcs, to get a good plot.  Dark Urge is the only true way to play the game as a custom character, its the only interesting plot in the whole game. Gale ruins the game by being in the party early, and then you find out he is the main character and he can just be god for no reason, breaking like 100 years of cannon lore of D&D.  The main plots, fine, at best, the set up is fine, low level is interesting enough. Starting from Act 2 it takes a nose dive. The big reveal at the end is terribly executed and the betrayal is complete nonsense. Why would someone who had spent 100 years fighting something just shrug and join that big bad cus you would rather kill the BBEG without killing yourself, so dumb.  Game play is baby easy, having infinite resources makes the game unplayable easy even on the highest difficulty. You must mod the game to make the game play enjoyable at all.  There is a very limiting class and subclass selection and overall player options. D&D has something like 100 subclass options and this game has like what 20 or so.  Is the game a good gateway drug for D&D, yes. Is this game the closest to a true 5E experience for video games, maybe. But the game is bad if you have played D&D for more than 6 months, and gets worse the longer you have played D&D. If you want a compelling story and you care about that, DO NOT PLAY THIS, go play Exp33 or something.

A great game overall in terms of production, but it's definitely a 21st Century Western game, with all that entails.  By trying to please everyone and make each NPC companion essentially whatever you want them to be in the name of "player choice", they've created characters that never find their footing. Shadowheart is particularly disappointing in her irresoluteness when it comes to the romance between her and the main character. One moment it's a love story about good triumphing over evil, the next it's ambivalence toward participation in a polyamorous orgy with people you met a minute ago.   I'd be fine with these characters if they had principles, if Gale was really a homosexual, or Shadowheart was really a polyamous homosexual, if Halsin was a homosexual... if Wyll was a homosexual... if Astarion was a homosexual. But Larian wants to please everyone so we're just supposed to forget that literally every male companion I've been nice to and had a friendship with wants to have sex with me because I've chosen not to relent to their advances. And advances they were, which just makes it all the more strange and taints every future encounter with these characters. It's fine if you want to make a game where everyone is homosexual, go for it. But I also reserve the right to not be able to relate to the characters at all, and that's where we're at.   It's a shame because many of the characters were very promising. Shadowheart is a great love interest. Gale is a charming, almost roguish wizard and very charismatic. Astarion is excellently acted. Wyll is also very likeable. But it's all tainted by this hamfisted sex sim gameplay that adds literally nothing to the game, especially when you realize that they will be and do whatever you want them to be and do if you click the right responses.  Take Cyberpunk2077 as a contrast. I liked Judy way more than Pamam. Yeah. Too bad. Judy told me straight up to get lost. She's a character written with intent, and CDPR didn't care that I liked Judy, they cared about what Judy wanted. That's the way Larian should have handled all of this, but in an effort to please everyone, they made their characters flimsy and ultimately two dementional. It's a real shame because the non-romance characters like the Devil or the Emperor or the Chosen are all superb, proving that Larian could have done this with your closest companions, but instead tainted them with this weird sex stuff that does nothing but distract. Would it have killed the PC if Shadowheart had simply had a bit of sensibility and refused to have a bisexual orgy with total strangers? I guess the takeaway is that everyone you interact with meaningfully in Baldur's Gate is a sex crazed pervert who is down for everything like they've been snorting lines all night. Uggh.  And that's really the biggest problem. The game is epic otherwise, with a lot to do and see, good gameplay, interesting quests, meaningful choices, and some great set piece battles. They really nailed it. But even with all of that, it will never compare to games that nailed their storytelling and characters above all, games like Dragon Age: Origins or even The Witcher 3.   Not for kids. Not recommended for everyone. You have to be a certain type of person for this game. Or you can just bite the bullet for the experience. Is it a really good game? Yes. Worth playing? Probably. But it's not a forever classic because it dated itself. It will always be a 2010-2020 game of the era.

I bought this game because of the massive hype around it, and unfortunately I can’t say I enjoyed it at all.  I was sold on the idea that you can “do whatever you want,” but in practice the game feels extremely restrictive. There were multiple situations where I wanted to do very simple, logical things, like assassinating someone or throwing a sleeping body off a nearby ledge, and the game just outright wouldn’t allow it. Often for no logical reason. Enemies can magically lock doors against you, yet casually run through them themselves. It creates a very caged, artificial feeling.  The flow of the game also feels unnatural. Most meaningful character interactions are forced in camp, marked by giant exclamation points, which completely kills immersion. The same goes for the romance system. Romance is constantly pushed into conversations where it doesn’t belong, often feeling awkward or outright uncomfortable. In my case, most of my camp disliked me, yet immediately after an argument, Gale suddenly acted romantically during what I thought was just a magic lesson. It felt disconnected, forced, and poorly handled.  Dialogue was another disappointment. Even after carefully going through dialogue options, the responses I actually wanted to say often just weren’t there. The game frequently boiled down to: run to NPC, fight, run to another NPC, fight, rest, then awkwardly walk around camp triggering forced interactions. None of it felt natural or immersive.  Graphically, the game is fine, decent, but nothing impressive. Voice acting is inconsistent: some actors do a great job, others really don’t. Overall, nothing stood out enough to make up for the gameplay and design issues.  In my opinion, this game was massively overhyped and ended up being a huge disappointment. I genuinely regret spending $50 on it, especially after saving up for something I thought I would love. Maybe with heavy modding my experience could have been better, but I mod games I already enjoy, not games I’m trying to fix. Baldur’s Gate 3 just wasn’t it for me...

Baldurs Gate 3 offers a vast and inviting world in the Dungeons and Dragons universe. You are empowered to choose your way to play this game. You have the option to approach a questline on different ways (negotiate, battle through hords of enemies or sometimes even skipping boss fights, stealth etc). The story is engaging and your allies and foes have well developed personalities. The world is enormous and detailed. Everything in Baldurs Gate 3 cries for endless oportunities and game of the year.  And truth to be said, that is the reason why I could not recommend it. This game is not for everyone, because it is so gigantic and overextending. It binds you for hundreds of hours in front of your computer. And as soon as you complete the game for the first time, you have to play it once again, because you will not grasp every facette of the game by playing it once. For many players this is no problem and will appeal them. You gain a lot of content for a good price. Hoever for a hard working person with de facto little to no free time, Baldurs Gate 3 is too much, too enormous and too long.

This might get the title of the most overrated game in history and it's not even close  Before I started playing the game I thought it was gonna be very similar to DnD, a game I'm a very big fan of... And it's nothing like that.  First of all, there is NO customization of the character except for the Aesthetic customizations, there are barely any subclasses with most of the classes only having 4 subclasses, which is almost none, having 4 subclasses means that one is useless, one is extremely situational and kinda bad even when it is supposed to be good, one is below average and the last one being the obviously best choice and should always pick that, and it's is ALWAYS like that, fighter, Monk, Paladin, Warlock, Barbarian, you name it. The Feats are no different either, I wanted to make an overly complicated build but there were no good feats to work with, and if they were good, then they weren't necessarily perfect for my build(s). Races are the worst ones tho only having the common races and Drows and Giths for some reason, a Firbolg or a Triton would be fire, but I guess we like focusing on the wrong stuff around here don't we?  None of there things would be difficult to implement, not even a little bit, I mean the Samurai subclass (one of the most iconic ones) literally just gives you advantage and that's it, what are we doing people?  The story and characters are just mid, they don't really feel like they have depth and most of them always act a certain way except for a few occasions where they straight up don't act like themselves, like when Astarion asked me to give him the parasite, when he's probably the least character (except for Laezel) who'd want to be controlled or influenced by such monstrosities. The story also almost forces you to play a specific way, or at least heavily implies that you should, all I wanted to do was to become as strong as possible without altering my body in harmful ways such as the permanent see invisibility eyeball, yet, when the game starts you're forced to be infected by said parasite and the game even heavily suggests to use it afterwards and will do anything to make you or somebody in your teams use the damn parasite, so I could throw my entire idea of a character into the trash; You're not influencing the story with your actions, It's the story that influences you if you dare to take actions the developers don't like or didn't plan for you to do, Glorious! Yes I know you CAN remove it much later into the game, but for a new player that has such a fantasy or is simply disgusted or gets uncomfortable knowing that a parasite is living in their head AND the game wants them to keep it and even feed it to get stronger, that's definitely not a good look, and it could've been completely avoided by just making the story just a lil different, but "Oy Vey don't you dare critique my story, you dirty HillyBilly"  Also yk how you can often choose what to say between dialogues? Oh yeah, that doesn't matter at all in 90% of conversations, seriously, I tested that often; 90% isn't the precise number but you get the point  The gameplay is probably the worst one tho, it's precisely the same as the story, where if you don't play precisely as the developers wanted, you're NOT gonna have a good time. In the beginning of the game you're given the freedom to choose what "storyline" to do next, and you do that by going in places where they'll give you said storyline mission and also where you'll spend some time in while completing said mission, however not all of them were made to be done immediately after finishing the tutorial (a terrible tutorial too), for example at a certain point I wanted to do the storyline of the hag, and a friend of mine told me not to because that's intended for players of a higher level than the one I was at the time (like lvl 3 or something), like FYM IT'S NOT INTENTED FOR NEW PLAYERS IT'S RIGHT BEHIND THE STARTING AREA OF THE GAME AFTER THE TUTORIAL. I beat her anyways cus I'm good at dnd but seriously how do new players enjoy ts? AND before that, I had already beaten, what I believe is supposed to be, the hardest act 1 mission, BRO IT WAS LITERALLY THE FIRST OR SECOND MISSION I ENCOUNTERED.  The developers WANTED YOU to play a specific way and they expected you to know what it was AND they'll punish you if you misbehave or just TAKE THE WRONG TURN, I DO NOT KNOW how newbies understand what they're supposed to do or how they deal with ts at all???  And since I know that I'm good at dnd I thought I could higher the difficulty, but guess what? EVERY. DAMN. DIFFICULTY. HAS EXCLUSIVELY INCREASED STATS COMPARED TO THE OTHER ONES WE'RE IN 2023 PEOPLE NOT IN 2003; you wanna know how much the stats increase between each difficulty? THEY GET DOUBLED, HAS ANYONE IN THIS DAMN TEAM OF DEVELOPERS PLAYED ANY VIDEOGAMES BEFORE, IN FACT, HAVE THEY EVER PLAYED DND AT ALL???  The social interactions might be the worst tho, I had a pretty good relationship w Astarion and sometimes he was just rude to me like my relationship with him was negative and I never understood why, in the meantime Shadowheart who was leaning to the negative and Laezel who was DEFINITELY negative both confessed OUT OF NOWHERE, I WASN'T EVEN CLOSE TO ACT 2 YET, not only that but Laezel ACTUALLY just came out of nowhere, more than 70% of our interactions were either passive aggressive, disagreeing, suspicious of each other or straight up negative in a way or in the other, yet when she confessed she said all positive things about me, LIKE BRO WHAT ARE YOU TALKING ABOUT??? WE NEVER HAD A POSITIVE CONVERSATION BEFORE? I shut off the game immediately after that and started playing it a few days later while avoiding to talk to her at all costs cus that just made me straight up uncomfortable. It's quite clear how the developers wanted the players to have segsy time w everyone like a nymphomaniac while doing whatever they want because seriously WTF was that? Straight up perverted bonobos, be better.  Then there are some other relatively minor problems that lowkey make the game unbearable, for example, the player's movement speed while outside of combat is SO SLOW, I had to get a mod to almost triple it, yet is still felt pretty slow, The Inventory and UI feel very, very bad and the fights are also very buggy, sometimes an attack gets advantage for no reason, sometimes it get disadvantage for no reason, especially with ranged attacks (and no, it's not me having a lack of knowledge on certain things, I know about highground, being too close to a target or being hidden, sometimes you just get advantage for the funsies), I'm also pretty sure that getting a 20 is lower than the other numbers, the only time I got a 20 was one time where I repeatedly reloaded a game file to see how much time it took to give me a nat 20 (it took between 30 and 40 minutes, not even joking)  Also I didn't even get to that part but apparently everyone (even the glazers of this game) absolutely hate act 3, and that should tell you a lot about what 1/3 of the whole game turn out to be, let's remember that that the majority of players who voted for ts to become Goty didn't even play or enjoy a whole Third of the game; that's tough.  Also the music is ok... I guess... it's not great per se, but it's the only part of the game that i wouldn't change or straight up remove  3/10 DID NOT deserve GOTY

The user interface is painfully bland for a fantasy game. It reuses the same UI as the character creation for every level up. The character design is so counterintuitive. You have a flamboyant flashy white haired guy as a rogue of all things. And, the wizard is charismatic and charming. Despite those being the literal opposite things you'd have for those classes.   I had an awful experience getting arrested in the early campaign with counterintuitive "warnings" that what I was doing was wrong. I was looting dead corpses, and the guard who wasn't even on my screen was giving me cryptic messages like, "danger is rising, not everything found is yours." And, somehow that translates to "hey, if you steal an arbitrary amount of items, I'll arrest the random character you have selected from a mile away."  Then I go and try to free my imprisoned character, and the game just bugs out and my character is glitched into the door just from me clicking on it. Permanently.   I also get this cool shapeshifting ability early into the game, and use it right before talking to the wizard. Effectively "changing my entire appearance to be unrecognizable." Then he flat out says "hey, I saw you on the ship." How can he recognize me from the ship when I'm shape shifted into a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ orc. This happened twice with another character who recognized me from the ship. Why are you adding cool abilities if they don't do anything??  So, to summarize. Characters are poorly designed, the writing is awful, skills are lackluster and boring, and customization is extremely limited. If you're in love with the mind-numbing template of D&D combined with characters that could've been designed by a 5 year old, with sex thrown in? Then you'll love this game.

I just did not enjoy it for some reason. It was a little too campy for me. Even came back a year later and tried the Dark Urge storyline and just couldn't. I WOULD NOT recommend it to other PLAYERS LIKE ME. I WOULD RECOMMEND it to most as an entry point to DnD or Isometric RPGs. I will absolutely say that after revisiting the game, I found a deep respect in how combat areas were designed for optimal freedom and encourage the use of your abilities in creative ways. Divinity was unique but Larian really started to explore this with Divinity:OS. You will like BG3 if you like stuff like Critical Role and creative turn-based rpg mechanics. I prefer the the characters and stories in the Owlcat games myself. (Wrath of the Righteous and beyond- the timer on Kingmaker turned me off.  It's a good blend of Larian's Talents and DnD's lore, but the writing felt shallow and catered to a broader audience.      This is my opinion, in no way do I believe it didn't deserve game of the year for its release. The budget put into it, the teams that worked together, and using the source material with the game itself opened up turn-based strategy to a wider audience. I was, however, released at the right time with the right publicity and IP- DnD was getting a resurgence through stranger things and the new DnD movie. Television shows were fantasy heavy. When I install 150GB  after over 100 hours in the game initially, play a few hours and decide to uninstall-it's just not for me. I previously enjoyed BG, BG2, NWN, NWN2, PF:WOTR, and Solasta just to name some bigger DnD/DnD inspired games in the same vein.

Get it if you can on sale but I personally don't believe it lives up to the hype.

Long story short: I really wanted and tried to like this game, but I just can't.  Now to elaborate: I had 44 hours in this game. I was still in Act I (in the underdark), and it took me that long because I geniuely wanted to give this game the best chance I felt it deserved. I explored, I read up lore, tried establishing relationships with characters and decided to suck it up and deal with combat system I don't particularly like. That effort was for naught.  I love Baldur's Gate 1 and 2, these are the crpgs dear to me. BG3 however, is not a Baldur's Gate game. Not only status quo in the game means Gorion's Ward entire journey was for nothing, for years I keept hearing how little respect this game offers to legacy characters. I think there might be point to it now. There is a reason why people talk about Shadowheart, Astarion and other Larian's OCs, and no one mentions Minsc or Jaheira. Or how no one talks about Sarevok or Viconia. On other things, the game doesn't really attempt to make me believe BG3, BG2 and B1 are taking place in the same timeline.  On it's own merit, it is 6/10 crpg. It doesn't do anything that haven't been done before in crpg genre, nor does it improve on it. Unfortunately, some basic things like exploring the world (you can move only one character at once or entire party, with nothing in between. Sometimes it is detrimental) or even inventory managament feels like a chore. The combat has all it should have to be enjoyable, but no kidding, Baldur's Gate 3 is the first crpg I have ever played that I found myself consistently procrastinating, when I knew combat encounter is coming.  I needed to be honest with myself, I just can't enjoy this game regardless how many chances I gave to it. I also don't understand what people see in it. And that's a shame, because as I said, I really would like to enjoy this game.

When it comes to video games, I try to rate them by my immediate thought when I finish them. In case of Baldur's Gate 3, my immediate thought was: "Finally, this is over.".   I played it two times - with a friend on release and alone after finale patch. In both ways I've got the same feeling.  I love crpg games. And I noticed how much inspiration developers took from Star Wars: Knight of the Old Republic and Dragon Age Origins. Mostly with story tropes and companions. Unfortunately, story and companions feel average compared to how they were in previously named games.   Even though my sympathy for tactical games - combat in Baldur's Gate 3 is frustrating and not fun.  To my conclusion, this game seems like a triple-A fantasy smut that is way too long.

[h1]NO - Until Game-Breaking Bugs & QoL Are Fixed[/h1]  This is a fantastic game brought down by some severe technical and design issues that can ruin the experience.  [u]Game-Breaking Bug (Act 3):[/u] The Stormshore Tabernacle has a bug that can permanently corrupt your save. The sequence:  1. Get [b]Anointed in Splendour[/b].  2. Destroy the donation chests.  3. Get the buff again with a [b]different character[/b].  After this, [b]all future saves will Crash to Desktop on load[/b]. It took me 4 hours to recover my playthrough. For many, this would be a total loss. [i]PLS FIX.[/i]  [u]Major Quality-of-Life Issues:[/u]  - [b]Abysmal Inventory:[/b] It lags, can crash the game, and is a nightmare to manage. As a loot goblin, not having nested containers or proper organization means you can spend 30 minutes searching for one item. Unacceptable for a game this loot-heavy.  - [b]Painfully Slow Combat:[/b] Late-game fights with dozens of low-HP enemies (e.g., 30 rats) are a slog. You are forced to watch each one "think" through its turn. A simple speed-up/skip animation toggle is desperately needed.  [hr][/hr] [h3]Suggestions:[/h3]  - Fix the Tabernacle null exception bug.  - Add a curse for damaging sacred chests.  - Make donations fixed-cost to stop respec meta-gaming.  - [b]Overhaul the inventory system.[/b] tho I get it's a big ask.  - Add a combat speed boost key.

You have to install a mod to be able to move around with WASD, and if you're an obsessive hoarder like me in these kinds of games, you MUST install a carry weight mod to be able to carry more than a bag of air.  The combat is ATROCIOUS. It's [b]extremely[/b] slow waiting for everything to happen, and you never seem to get involved in fair fights. You could just be walking along, minding your own business, and suddenly everyone and their dog has decided to get into a fight with you. Then there's the rolls, my god, there is absolutely no way the chances to hit are actually what it says. When it says 55%, it's probably more like 20-25%, and when it says 95%, well, to miss several of those in a row you'd think would be rare... but it isn't. Then, even if you DO manage to hit more than 50% of the time, you also get screwed on rolls for damage too, so even that isn't fair. It's RNG on RNG on RNG - just not fun.  Even getting new, better items doesn't feel meaningful. Item rarity seems to be almost completely meaningless. I never feel like I'm getting stronger.  I really wanted to like it, and I will attempt to persevere, but everything just feels so unfair ALL THE TIME. It's like I'm roleplaying as a victim.  Oh, and what's with calling the grub-looking things "tadpoles"? Has Larian ever seen a tadpole? It's very obviously [i]not[/i] a tadpole. It's a grub.

I know I am supposed to really love this game, and for someone who've put more than 500 hours into Divinity Original Sin 2, I should really be into this.... but I'm not.   First of, the beginning felt like a 1-to-1 copy from Divinity.  Divinity: Remove collar. BG3: Get rid of the parasite. Both permament "states" you want to rid yourself of, though the parasite has more in-game effect. Divinity: You start on a ship. BG3 you start on a space ship. Same thing reskinned.   These are some of my personal reasons to not recommend this game: - I simply dont care about whatever they got going on. Every character feels so over-the-top and not in an interesting way - I have to watch every conversation, even a random NPC saying a one-liner. I am put into place and camera switch just to listen to that random generic nonsense... every... time. - I dont have the same freedom of movement as in Divinity, and all spells just feels the same with do 0.x% more damage or whatever. Even the icons are so lazy designed that I constantly had issues differing the actions, so had to read the tooltip. Unlike in Divinity where the icons are easy to differ instantly, as they are unique and not just the same button with the same effect but with a new similar icon . - The action, bonus action and all that. Too complicated without bringing depth. In Divinity I always know, how far I could go, what I could do, planned everything. In BG3 I was pointing with the cursor to understand what the hell the game is trying to tell me, are you walking? you wanna go up/down there? well who cares, the game picks whatever. So many quickloads because of missclicks as the UI isnt elaborating anything.  - The entire "hub" thing re-titled as camp was the most annoying part. The "hub" thing ruined "Shadow Gambit" for me, even though I played Shadow Tactics and Desperados 3 for countless hours. Same with the Sniper Ghost series, the last entry (3) had a hub, nobody liked it - but for some reason we should say its good here? Why? Its not - The D&D thing? I dont care about none of that, never played fantasy board games, and have no interest in it. I bought a video game, and wanted to play a video game, not a board game, yet felt like a hybrid leaning too much towards the board game. Even Larian have stated that this brought too many restrictions and didnt match their vision, and that is why I have high hopes for the new Divinity. I dont like this game but I still like Larian. - I killed the vampire dude simply because he annoyed me like crazy. Wanted to kill them all, but no Lone Wolf like in Divinity, I am forced to play in a party which I have zero interest in. Micromanaging everyones feelings and personal nonsense. Cut their throat, loot them, move on. That is not possible here, so it feels like a major downgrade from their previous title for those that want to play that way.  - Constantly blah blah nonsense from NPCs, and people I have no reason to help except for xp and loot. - Shadowheart left my camp in anger because I did whatever thing, cant even remember what she god pissed over and dont really care, but she had TONS of items as a mule, and there were no possible way for me to retreat them, as my closest saves were locked to a place where I couldnt go to camp. Thats where she was in quarantine for nagging, but couldnt get any of my stuff back so bye bye because little miss crybaby didnt like me. At least give me the option to fight her so I can kill her and take my loot back. That was just tedious for the sake of being tedious.  Was the writing good? Absolutely! The conversations, those that mattered, were amazingly well done. But for me that was not enough to save all the stuff I hated. Im really pissed because I wanted to like this game, but after trying to force myself for countless hours I gave up.  I have high hopes for the new Divinity game. And at least this gave me a reason to replay Divinity, to experience the enjoyment I was looking for here but never found.

I wouldn't recommend this game simply because there seems to be a blind acceptance of its heavy satanically driven content.    It desperately encourages the character to accept, which most do, transhumanism, much like Cyberpunk 2077 and numerous other corporate void games that have little in the way of meaning or morality.    It attempts to force characters and thus the player, to accept devils and demons as everyday companions - it removes the nature of morality altogether - I found that I could hack my way through a town with little or no consequence to even my public reputation. The fundamental philosophy of the game is skewed and flawed in ways that prevent the player from even attempting a basic morality.    Next are the mechanical failures of the game, which I am surprised did not bother players more - specifically the issues with the camera, which has difficulty in various areas staying on the same elevation as the player, so you are forced into a birds eye view to correct the issue. Jumping also has problems and I really didn't understand why I couldn't use rope to cross chasms.    Then there is the abandonment of the original BG series - mechanically you are no longer afforded the pleasure of live turn based combat, which is vastly superior gameplay in contrast with strict turn based gameplay. I understand that because of online group dynamics such a feat would me more challenging if a pause feature could not be implemented for groups, which it could, but that is all to say the combat system is lazy and unimaginative. If I wanted to play Original Sin I would, and I don't. Some of the longer or repeat combat scenarios are BORING!    Then comes the frantic fleeing from the best part of the BG series, character creation and classic D&D mechanics, which if you did not play D&D 35 years ago may not seem like anything important but the differences are. I do ever so hate the even ended stats system - the fact is that there was always supposed to be a great deal of variance in player stats to make the game more realistic, without the player role (diced based stat determination) in character creation its just not as fun, more bland and sterilized like the rest of the gameplay.    The characters themselves all seem to be fatherless degenerates, likely reflecting the personalities of whoever made this game - everyone is angry and bloodthirsty - and if they are righteously minded then you can be sure they have horns or are a l3sbian or something.    I did not finish the game (I got to Act III, left it running so my hours are astronomical) but the initial storyline of the brain worms was not bad but kind of desperate in its attempts to explain every solution away whilst setting up contradicting forces on each side of the player which made me feel more like a mediator in other peoples affairs for each decision forced on me.    I didn't care about any character in the game, if they all had died it would not have affect the story or gameplay for me personally - in BG I & II however I did become attached to PCs in terms of their personal stories and what they added to my party (what is with the 4 person party size limit btw?). Jeheira's fight in and out of the Harpers or Jan's family issues or whatever were just more realistic that these stupid "I'm enslaved to a demon god, If I die the whole world will be destroyed" storylines that do not relate to a normal person. I can relate to Judge Dredd more than Gale or Astarion.    And by far the most assaulting and disturbing problem with BG3 is the frequent early attempts to force my character into a h*mos*xual relationship. I know most new games were woke, but children are definitely playing this game and the horrifying ease by which you can unknowingly choose a dialogue which transitions quickly to g*y p*rn is truly ungodly.    Disgusting.

I'd heard so much praise about this game, I finally decided to give it a try. I've been playing a lot of immersive, story-rich RPGs: Kingdom Come 2, a replay of The Witcher 3, etc. Everyone kept telling me that I had to play BG3. I'd been hoping it would go on sale but finally gave in to the hype and bought it at full price.  And I kind of hate it?   I kept hearing everyone say how immersive it is, but I find it to be the opposite of immersion. That's due in large part to the classic RPG style, and how I'm constantly having to interact with very specific menus and systems and inventories in a way that almost becomes invisible in other games. That's not to my taste, but I can't fault the game for being what it is.  The other reason I have a hard time with immersion is that your character doesn't speak during dialogue. This makes every conversation feel awkward, and I never get a feel for my character actually existing. They're just an avatar. Which, yes, I know all video game characters are in a literal sense. But most games allow me to feel that the player character has a personality and wants and hopes, largely from the performance of the voice actor, and that informs the decisions I make while playing that character. Without their dialogue, it doesn't ever feel like they actually exist. Which again breaks immersion for me. And also makes me wonder why we even had the option of choosing a voice for them in the first place.  The enemy AI is very, very stupid. Maybe this is because I just played Kingdom Come 2, where NPCs will notice a door in their house open at night and so look around to see if anything is missing, then remember you were snooping around and come look for you. But I've had several encounters in BG3 where during the fight I just close a door on half the enemies and, instead of opening it, they just shoot arrows at the closed doors while I kill their friends.  I just finished Act One, so maybe this gets better, but I also feel like the choices made and the game's overall reactivity aren't very good. I'd heard your choices matter so much in this game, but NPCs will tell me things I never did, make assumptions over big choices where I chose differently, and even tell me we'd made plans together earlier when this is the first time I'm meeting them.  The voice acting is pretty stellar for the NPCs, but the writing is lackluster and juvenile. I can get past that to a certain extent (I love the original VtM Bloodlines, for instance, so some cheesy writing and jank can be fun) but it just runs counter to my expectations for the game due to all the praise it received. I find the characters to be pretty two dimensional. And for all the different races and classes in the game, there's no sense of "culture" among the different groups. The Tieflings live and speak and act exactly like the humans and the halflings and so on. Only the Goblins (enemy NPCs) seem distinct in any way.  It's also very combat heavy. I try to find a way around combat as much as I can, but most encounters force you into combat, even though my stealth and social stats are very high. I'd hoped for more choice and variation.  I'm hoping to find a way to appreciate the game more, but I am pretty baffled at all the praise and awards it's received. It's not an awful game by any means, but it also comes nowhere near the praise it's received.  tl;dr - Make sure you know what kind of game to expect when playing this and that it's the type of game you're into before buying, and don't let the praise make you think you're getting something on the level of the best games you've ever played. My dislike of this game is not just due to its faults but also my expectations before playing it.

Clearly a lot of people really like this game and that is great, however for me personally, despite having put over 100 hours in; trying different combinations of class and race hoping that something would click and also watching a number of guides and trying things out, it just hasn't caught my imagination.  The main things that have turned me away are:  1. Turn based combat.  I'm not against turn based gameplay in things like strategy games where you controlling things at a higher or perhaps broader level, but for this type of game, it makes combat feel slow and clunky.  That may just be because I associate this style of game with an action RPG and so my expectation is for real-time combat.  2.  Dice roll RNG.  I think for table top D&D using dice does makes sense.  However, whilst there are rules in place, table top involves real people and the DM and players can use their imagination to work around the results of dice throws in order to keep the story moving.  In game, there are a finite number of outcomes that can be programmed and all that ends up happening is either the outcome ends up being the same regardless of the route you take to get there, or you permanently lose out on something or you end up resorting to saving and reloading until you get the result you want.  3. Having to control all party members. At the start of the game you get to create a single character. This can take some time, the result of which means you feel a connection and investment in that character.  However, once the game gets going you begin to pick up new party members.  Those are pre-made characters who you don't really have an investment in but are expected to level and control all the same.  I would have preferred if either you could create all the characters yourself (which wouldn't work as the story revolves around the game knowing their backstories and they are obviously in the cut scenes), or there was an option for the computer to control those characters leaving you to focus on your own character.  4. Due to the nature of this being an implementation of table top D&D, movement is restricted.  This applies both inside and outside of combat. However, outside of combat those restrictions feel, well, restrictive. Characters move at what feels like snail pace. Moving through water slows a character down even further, which seems a bit pointless when outside of combat.  Even when terrain clearly looks like it could easily be traversed, you may not be able move in that direction (including jumping) because it is outside of the allowed path.  For me, this breaks the immersion.  5. Characters in your party can be left behind.  Occasionally, a party member will get stuck or just not have the available movement to stay with the party.  If that happens it is up to you to notice and go back for them or swap to that character to bring them forwards.  If the party members are selected to be grouped then I expect them to either keep together or for some notification to be given to let you know if one is left behind.  It's a small gripe, but irritating none the less.  6. Genitalia. I realise D&D is meant to be a game of imagination but the inclusion of genitalia in an RPG computer game, rather than seeming clever, funny or innovative, just feels a little 'oh dear, how sad'.  There is an option to turn it off at least.

I'm done. I may change my mind later. Right now, I have had enough of being dragged from one encounter to the next without any explanation or understanding as to why I should care or comply. The combat is mediocre, the buildcraft banal, the interface unintelligible, the pacing panicked, the writing juvenile, the worldbuilding nonsensical, the art directionless, and the characters cardboard. I put 200+ unenjoyable hours into trying to figure out this critically acclaimed game's narrative and ludic languages, looking for some hint of a hook or emotional payoff to suggest that I'm not wasting my time. Nothing has revealed itself.  I believe what BG3 is going for is the experience of playing a tabletop RPG. The limited and missing details of the world feel like the limitations of a game master describing the important parts and shepherding you to the next part of the adventure. What I have seen, however, reminds me of the worst parts of Mass Effect; being forced into stupid decisions and conflicts for plot.  On top of that, you have tabletop RPG conventions mixing poorly with computer game conventions. The roll of the die is meant to be the focal point of your efforts. I'm sure it's quite compelling to someone who enjoys gambling and games of chance. To me, it's an animation that plays before I find out if i have to reload or not because my 18 charisma expert persuader rolled a 1. And if I'm going to have to reload anyway, I might as well just automatically succeed at every roll regardless of ability and proficiency, since that would be the only result I ever accept. Thus the entire construct breaks down.  I've never watched Critical Role. I bounced off Vox Machina. What I have done is read the entirety of Discworld, the main parts of Dune, and a lot of Robert E. Howard's short stories and poetry. So maybe this game was a mistake to begin with for me. But that also means I can't think of anybody I would recommend it to. So for now, I just don't.  TLDR just play Fallout New Vegas instead.

This is the first CRPG I've played where I had the impression that the game might notify law enforcement of choices made in game. There is something unsettling about it. There is very obviously a "correct" way to play the game, and an "incorrect" way. [b]I did [u]not[/u] care for the 120GB download.[/b]  That assessment may be at least a partial reflection of the fact BG3 is significantly different from BG1 and 2, grognard discomfort with change, etc. But while there seems to be lots of "choices and consequences" I have the distinct impression there isn't actually much flexibility. In fairness, the 'chosen one' story kind of precludes that, but that kind of story always irks me. And it's blasted into the user's face immediately! BG1 had a much better approach - didn't really get a sense of the player character's role in the plot until later in the game (Of course, Arcanum has the best version of the 'chosen one' story imo).   Also, very PO'd that there was no players' guide or equivalent in game. Owlcat did this well in their Pathfinder games, so there was no excuse for BG3 to exclude that information on character advancement, spells, etc. Even the OG BG1 and 2 let you look up those details. Going from 6 to 4 characters is a little lame but not a dealbreaker; however I do not care for the lack of alignment associated with characters. Perhaps that's how D&D plays now but it's stupid and lazy. Don't care for the moral relativism implied and it obviously causes problems with not-even-remotely-relativistic classes like paladins - yes, the paladin oath system is stupid. Lack of alignment obviously neuters squabbles between clerics and the pantheon too. Again, maybe thats a D&D thing now and not the studio's interpretation, in which case nu-D&D or whatever [strike]sucks sooo muchhh[/strike][b]has lost its way[/b].  BG3 is very strong in presentation though - the game runs smoothly, looks good (mostly), and to be honest it would probably be a great engine for modders to build their own campaigns on, but this ain't Neverwinter either. Pity. One area where it's presentation fails is the music and soundtrack. There is nothing dynamic about anything I've heard thus far, which is unfortunate as both BG1 and 2 had some very memorable themes.  I haven't finished the game but also may or may not (at present, wandering around Moonrise Towers) - it just feels too locked up to me. I'll give a specific example here, and if i can actually do what I am attempting to do I'll edit this review accordingly to eat crow. I am attempting to play off the various players introduced around the Moonrise Towers off against each other, like a fantasy Yojimbo. I suspect I will be forced to "choose a side" regardless, at which point I may just give up on BG3. We'll see.   (I was already quite annoyed by the game's direction rubbing my nose in my resolution of the first set piece. Thanks, I know what the player character decided to do, [b]"haha no look at what has been done, revel in it, isn't this what you wanted, player?"[/b] No, actually they were all useless to my purpose, but [i]gaem no allow go forwards adventure until done big-jig choice[/i].)  Disappointed grognard noises.

Unpopular opinion time. I didn't like Divinity either, so this being a stinker isn't really that much of a shock. 6 hours in and the combat is still a badly unbalanced railroad. Cause and effect are nonsensical; seeing the druid camp explode into an everyone versus everyone riot is beyond illogical. I have zero investment in the party characters, with them all being so fundamentally dislikeable. The idea that 1st level characters have deeply rooted ties to great powers makes no sense either. Combat is decidedly uninteresting as a melee character, which leans the game into being a bard/mage playthrough as not only the default but pretty much mandatory option. Puerile writing, graphics and animation might interest some, but do nothing for the plot or believability of the characters here. Skill checks that if you don't have your character build and party exactly right, at the right time that cannot be adapted to (e.g. the necessary fight by the guardian statues in the Druid Camp). Tiny nods to the legendary BG1, 2 games are welcome; but offer little solace for a game that just isn't terribly much fun to play.    I keep trying to come back to this for the sake of playing out the story, but I'd probably be better served by just watching a playthrough on youtube. A shame, given the rave reviews and following this title has. But I just don't like Larian's style. Maybe other developers will get inspired to do something in this space again?

If you aren’t already a fan of D&D or turn-based combat, this game may not be your cup of tea. Personally, that side of it never really clicked with me. I’m not into turn-based games, and if I were playing solo, this would’ve been a hard no. I only stuck with it because I was playing with a good friend group.  The major downside: a significant amount of my total playtime has been replaying the same content due to bugs. Early on we were using mods, which may have contributed—but it’s worth noting that one player in our group ran the exact same mods and completed the entire game without issue. Despite that, multiple saves were corrupted or broken. Around Act 1 we removed all mods entirely just to preserve progress. Upon searching for resolutions to these issues, I find that there are thousands of other posts about this- and they're years old. This stuff should have been fixed, and its clear now that it never will be.   Act 2 was mostly stable, although we did encounter issues, it wasn't as bad, but once we hit Act 3, the wheels came off again. Right at the start of Act 3 we encountered a game-breaking roadblock that forced us to replay from an older save again. A few hours later, we hit another game-breaking issue and lost even more progress.  The actual gameplay, at its core, is good. The story is entertaining, and the ability to make choices and see different reactions from characters is genuinely well done. I went in completely blind, rolled a random character, and just committed to it—and I loved that part. It’s easily the least likely build I would’ve chosen on purpose, yet it led to some of the best moments we’ve had. That said, all of that good content is viciously undermined by the sheer volume of bugs.  For a $60 Triple-A game from a big name studio, the amount of serious bugs is honestly shocking. Steam’s two-hour refund window means you won’t discover these issues until it’s far too late to get your money back.  I have literally played $10 pre-release alpha games that aren't as buggy as this! The fact that this game is still sold at full price in this state is mind boggling.

I had to give up on this game. It's so disappointing to say that it just doesn't click for me. I loved DOS:II and when I learned that the same studio was making a licensed D&D game with the 5e rules that I had been using in my home games for years, I was ecstatic.  I stayed away from spoilers and waited for the full release of the game, but when it finally came out I wasn't impressed. What works in 5e often works because of the looseness with how rules are enforced at the table. What doesn't work in 5e is really brought to light when you have to interface with it in such an impersonal way. And what changed from 5e is usually for the better, but I also often find it puzzling. While there are many examples, one that hurts the most for me is how they gutted mage hand. In 5e, it's practically minor telekinesis (which was a thing in DOS:II that all characters could easily access), but in BG3 it's a once-per-day summon with massively reduced utility because of its cost to use. Again and again I find what excited me about playing a D&D game had been limited or removed entirely and replaced with systems that felt better suited for a different game license.  I have tried and tried and tried to enjoy BG3, but I can't even get through act 1 without feeling like it's a shadow of what it could have been. Which also makes me feel crazy considering how much everyone else loves it! How can I not recommend one of the greatest games in years that bucks the trends so many other studios have followed out of greed?  I so badly wanted to love BG3 as much as I did DOS:II. Hopefully the next game from Larian can do that, because I've given this one enough chances. Similarly, I've stopped playing D&D in favor of other RPG systems (Draw Steel, primarily), and so the original draw of 5e rules has nearly become a deterrent.  I'm not recommending, but it's not just because it didn't meet my expectations. I'm not recommending because I don't think that the gameplay is fun. I think looting bodies hurts the pacing of exploration. I think abilities feel too clunky to use. I hate casting mage armor and a bunch of ritual spells every time I rest, when tabletop D&D would resolve that with "I spend some time casting my buffs after every long rest" and never bringing it up again. Any damage from accidently pathing through hazardous terrain is a slap in the face when any DM would assume you take a safer route when it's easily available or let you know that you have to walk through fire if you go that way. I have to stop here because my list of gripes would go on forever and I would accomplish nothing but making myself angrier by thinking about this game any longer.

A great game and a horrible game at the same time.  Tactical combat was the highlight to me. The D&D spells coming alive, the fighting maneuvers, items, and equipment. Very well implemented. Maps are huge, with a lot to see and do and the game seems like a limitless possibility.  The problem is that the story is not interesting. It is presented through unsufferable, self-centered companions that can talk endlessly about their feelings and traumas. There are more DEI NPC’s and girl bosses than in a Netflix series. This can be avoided at first since the focus is on combat and exploration, but at the end of act 2, the plot is forced down your throat by endless expositions and virtue signaling that would make Sweet Baby Inc. very, very proud.   Sadly, the woke factor reached a critical point for me and playing the game became a chore, never finished it.

I was excited to play this game, considering all the hype and praise. Unfortunatley, while very expansive, the game is limited by the very strict DnD ruleset, that ends up making the game combat dull, and too much of the game progress reliant on chance rolls of the dice (skill checks).    Combat makes up alot of the game, but due to the strict adherence to the DnD ruleset, you end up being very limited in the choice of spells available for magic casters. The most powerful spells require "concentration", which means throughout the game your spell casters will cast just one powerful spell, and then hide from enemies so as to not have their "concentration" broken.    The abundance of locks that block the progress of the game means that in every playthrough, the player is forced to include a thief character in their party. Such characters are very weak combat-wise and do not pull their weight.    One of the joys of adventure games is supposed to be the upgrade in armour and weapons as one progresses. The game provides very little scope in that regard. My characters all had the same gear from level 7 up until the level cap of 12.    The crafting mechanic is so simple and primitive, it could have been left out of the game entirely without affecting the quality of gameplay.    The maps are poorly designed. 40% of the areas of the maps displayed in each act are impossible to explore or access. I realize this game is the golden child of the internet but given how much I am despising the time I am forced to play it with my friends, which is an arrangement I will NOT put up with much longer, I need the vindication of putting everything I despise about it on blast here.     getting the good parts out of the way: there are some nice quality of life features like being able to respec your entire build whenever you want. the environments are pretty, and so are the characters... sometimes. it's unfortunately rare. most of them are kinda ugly.     to begin with while it seems like you have a lot of options in combat in reality the most effective strategies are usually spamming the handful of moves over and over. there was no real attempt whatsoever to make interesting gameplay into good strategy. the open hand monk is the epitome of this insane design: the most powerful single target damage dealer in the entire game... hits things. and that's it. the other classes aren't much better. my druid friend who presumably has more spells than me is still only using the same 2-3 of them over and over in every encounter. the real heart of this problem is the stupid action system. you get one action and one bonus action per turn (assuming no special bonuses) and this just limits everything you can do so much that there really isn't any point in not maximizing your damage per turn. the DOS games have a much better system so I can only assume this idiocy was put in to match D&D 5e.     Almost all of the characters you recruit are actively hostile toward you. halsin, karlach, and wyll are the only ones who you aren't better off just killing right away narratively (for gods sake the greenskin woman literally wants to enslave you at first) yet the game treats you like a villain for doing so by cutting off entire swathes of content. I get that's the consequence for killing a party member, not having their questline, but if they were going to do things that way why not make more of the party actually, you know, likeable right away? or at the very least not directly hostile toward you? I keep thinking the further I go along that my friends are making a mistake by not killing a vampire who tried to eat them in their sleep or kicking out the green woman who is all kinds of a bad idea, but the game says otherwise. I guess I should make friends with more serial killers according to this nonsense.     the game is chock full of pointless loot that you can sell for pointless gold because all of the best armor and equipment comes from enemies and quest lines anyway. you can buy potions and scrolls which are kinda useful sometimes but that's it, and it's not nearly enough to justify how much junk is lying around. all these chests and barrels and interactables that exist solely to waste your time for the sake of immersion is antithema to me. it makes me feel like this game is about plowing through someone else's junkyard that we just happened to land in.     the combat scenarios are alright in act 1 though I never genuinely had fun with any of them, but in act 2 it's just one heaping pile of horse manure after the other. there is a fight where you need to defend an NPC, but said NPC is suicidal and constantly being bumrushed by flying monsters while also assaulted by a boss that does enough damage to kill her in two turns. there is a random surprise fight where the monsters explode without any warning if you kill them, so if you're a melee character enjoy reloading your save over a completely unpredictable outcome. there is a different boss fight with a guy who keeps spamming a defense move that means no one can attack him unless it's indirectly and does enough damage in one turn to kill any single member of your party. NONE OF THIS IS FUN TO DEAL WITH. it's like when they got to act 2 the entirety of the design team thought "I love pulling teeth out of my own head with no anesthetic and wish to simulate that in a video game". I hear act 3 is even worse but frankly I probably won't make it that far.     and something I completely forgot to mention is that larian the developers won't even let you truly create your own character. they forewent the addition of sliders because, and I quote, "Most people just make the same character". and that is the essence of everything I hate about this game distilled into one direct quote by the developer themselves: you can have choices, as long as it's the ones we approve of. Not Recommended.    The characters and story are great — genuinely interesting, and I really want to keep playing to see what happens next.    But the gameplay feels like it assumes you already know D&D rules. The game barely teaches its own mechanics, so if you’re new to this system everything becomes trial-and-error guessing: who is strong vs weak, which items are rare vs just cheap consumables, what you should save, and what you can freely spend.    Wizard leveling is especially confusing: you’re asked to pick spells without really understanding what they actually do in real combat. On top of that, healing spells suddenly run out — and at that point your whole party can just wipe in a “normal” fight.    And the worst part: it constantly feels like the game is punishing you for everything. For not knowing mechanics, for making “wrong” choices you couldn’t realistically understand, for exploring, for fighting, for leveling — for basically playing the game as a newcomer. There’s no satisfaction in that, only frustration.    It honestly feels like a card game where everyone knows the rules except you. As a newcomer, it’s painful and stressful even to fight on the first map.
