I'm pretty late to the BG3 party, and a game with an "overwhelmingly positive" rating like this one sets up some expectations. I don't expect any game to be perfect in all aspects, but I certainly wouldn't expect one with this rating to be as annoying as I found it to be.  Oh there's plenty of player choice, and the writers made sure there are plenty of options and paths to take. Great... now could I get party AI that doesn't walk into traps, so I don't have to constantly F5 and then F8 their stupid mistakes? Hell, sometimes they just get stuck, and I've found myself starting fights with one party member being across the map, because they failed to enter a lift with everyone else. One particular scenario really stuck in my mind when, right after a fight, the stupid AI walked into a leftover Cloud of Daggers and just... stood there.  Or how the game doesn't really give you enough information. Granted, perhaps I missed a memo, but I'd never expect "detect thoughts" to be something invasive (and it's never presented as such). Yet when I failed a skill check on that suddenly everyone in the vicinity became hostile - a bit of warning would have been nice, now it's an immediate F8. Speaking of "not enough information", the UI is atrocious; it was a good number of hours before I found how I can manually stop concentrating on active spells. But the UI being unintuitive isn't the worst thing on this list, since once you figure it out it's a done deal (i.e. it's a flaw, but not a constant one).  Want more constant annoyances? Inventory management. The game world is littered with items. From boxes, chests, burlap sacks, bookshelves, vases - the number of containers is staggering. And each such container can potentially have useful stuff inside. Pressing ALT should highlight only meaningful containers, but I found that's not really the case 100% of the time, and I've had containers with useful stuff (healing potions, gold, trap disarm kits, spell scrolls) ALT didn't show. So, the player has a choice - either engage with the tedious system of looking through each such container... or ignore them, knowing you're going to get less gold, miss spells, healing potions, arrows, etc... And then the actual management of your items... imagine the overly simplistic system from Skyrim but now you're carrying way more junk (potions, grenades, alchemical reagents, tools, food, armour, weapons, arrows, multiple story items, keys, oh-so-many-scrolls, valuables and anything-that-seemed-interesting-but-might-never-have-a-use) AND you have to manage FOUR such inventories at the same time.  Or how about conversations... NPCs can group up in conversations (i.e. you're talking to more than one NPC at a time). But only one person from the party ever speaks and it's only that character attribute scores that get used for skill checks. Which is a bit odd, because there's no telling what kind of encounter you're going to get; so ideally, you want all conversations to be started by the most charismatic character. If your avatar isn't that - tough luck and best remember to always switch to the party member with those charisma stats every time you want to start a convo! Oh, you forgot? And it's an important conversation? Well, F8 it is.  Or skill checks in scenarios which shouldn't need skill checking at all. A rule of thumb I remember back from my DMing days was to never require skill checks when there's no cost to doing something - like if a character can reasonably make multiple attempts without any cost, and if timing isn't a factor. Here this old PnPRPG rule is frequently ignored.  Now, all that said - the game has a ton of content to explore. And if the above gripes sound like something you can ignore and get past then getting the game is a no-brainer. But for me playing this title is an exercise in near constant frustration. Is the game good? Probably. Am I, personally, having fun? No, not really, no. Or, nowhere near what an "overwhelmingly positive" game would suggest.

I really wanted to enjoy Baldur’s Gate 3, and for a while, I genuinely did. The first half of Act 1 was rough enough that I almost stopped playing, but the second half of Act 1 was truly enjoyable. The pacing improved, the encounters felt fair, and for a moment it felt like the game completely understood what players want. I was genuinely glad I stuck with it. Unfortunately, that momentum didn’t continue. Technical Issues My issues started immediately with the Vulkan graphics option. Choosing Vulkan caused most of my mouse clicks to fail to register, making the game nearly unplayable. There’s no warning about this known problem, and the only fix was switching to DirectX 11. For a game that’s been out for a year, it’s surprising this hasn’t been addressed. Even after switching, mouse interactions remained inconsistent. Clicking objects often required pixel‑perfect precision, and companions frequently stepped in front of interactable items, interrupting actions or triggering conversations. Inventory and bag management made things worse—organizing bags felt pointless because vendors ignore your setup, reshuffle items, and create further clutter. Nested bags also created buggy behavior when stored inside your camp chest. The camera system added more frustration. It often clips into ceilings and walls, especially in tight rooms or when navigating stairs, making exploration uncomfortable and disorienting. Spellcasting and Targeting Issues Targeting is extremely sensitive. You can clearly position the cursor over an enemy or object—with the correct targeting indicator showing—but even the slightest movement of the mouse before clicking can cause the target to slip off. This leads to miscasts, or abilities firing at the ground instead of at the intended target. These issues seem tied to the inconsistent mouse‑click registration found throughout the game. Sometimes spells simply refuse to cast, with no feedback explaining why. Gameplay Design Concerns Some early-game encounters felt unexpectedly punishing due to autosave placement or lack of warnings. Dropping into an early temple immediately autosaves and locks you into combat with higher‑level enemies, forcing you to reload a much older save if things go wrong. This pattern repeats throughout the game, and inconsistent autosave locations caused frequent and frustrating losses of progress. While late Act 1 was genuinely great, Act 2 immediately regresses into difficulty spikes and unpredictable encounter triggers. Many events start combat from seemingly harmless interactions, and several fights rely on very specific setups the game doesn’t communicate ahead of time. Some mechanics—such as unavoidable curses or extremely large enemy swarms—felt more frustrating than strategic. Area‑of‑effect abilities also felt underpowered or too limited in range, which became a problem when facing large groups of enemies. Resting and Resource System The long‑rest system requires camp supplies, but gathering them becomes tedious. Containers are everywhere, but only a small fraction contain anything useful, and there’s no streamlined way to loot them. Expect to spend a lot of time opening nearly empty crates and barrels just to replenish spells. Positives Despite everything, the game has real strengths. The graphics are fantastic. The story and characters are genuinely impressive. When everything works, combat can be fun and engaging. There are moments—especially in the latter half of Act 1—where Baldur’s Gate 3 feels like something truly special. Conclusion I sincerely want to recommend Baldur’s Gate 3. It does have a lot of good qualities, and parts of it are excellent. But the technical problems, inconsistent difficulty, camera issues, and frustrating design decisions—many of which become more prominent in Act 2—make the experience difficult to fully enjoy. The game feels like it needed more time in development to polish its systems and fix long‑standing issues. Unfortunately, since it has already been out for a year, I’m not sure these problems will be addressed. For players willing to push through its rough edges, there are memorable moments and strong storytelling. But for me, the frustrations ultimately outweighed the fun.

So unbelievably overhyped and not at all worth that constant $60 pricetag.  Edit if you want an actual response, holy paragraphs Batman. Apparently comments are limited to 1000 characters or this would've been a reply. TDLR: It's not really an RPG.  It's hyped because it's a CRPG with motion capture and a fully voice acted cast, yeah I'm sure you and everyone else love seeing shadowheart rip off her clothes in 4k HDR.  I didn't feel like writing a whole essay for my original review but I already made this response and I think a game like this deserves some elaboration. I appreciate anyone willing to put up with my opinion, I know this is alot of people's favorite game, and if someone made a review like this about Clair Obscur I would be mad too. Don't forget that you are ABSOLUTELY NOT allowed to criticize art in any way, ESPECIALLY not popular art that everyone likes, or that makes you a bad gamer and that's an irredeemable sin. I've actually never liked any video game ever, in fact I hate all of them and wish my hobby would just die already. With that in mind, here's what I dislike about the game:  Every character is written with MCU dialogue. Everyone is witty and funny and "we're all going on an adventure team!" and it's so boring. I remember specifically Shadowheart saying "I guess that's what I like about you, you're different." Like seriously? My silent protagonist that has done nothing but stare and pick dialogue option number one the entire time is special? Why? Why am I different, literally tell me what I did, all we've done is battle and talk to people. It's just cringe all around, all the characters are cringe. That one annoying devil that just showed up and kidnapped me, then started speaking in poems as he tried to convince me he "totally didn't want my soul for bad things, only good things." Can you try at all? Or Gale who makes no attempt to come as someone you would even want in your party? Astarion didn't even join me until I needed a fourth member. Look at Ifan from Divinity Orginal Sin 2, he's immediately interesting. When you meet on the boat he's personable but still kinda mean, you can see there's more beneath the surface instantly. He's always meeting mysterious people doing very hush hush things, you want to know what the big secret is. I don't care about any of the companions or their secrets in BG3. Why should I? I can't interact with them like they interact with me, and most of them are really annoying anyway. I didn't meet a single character during my gameplay that I thought "Oh wow, you're really cool, whether you're a companion or not I like your voice acting, story, and design so much that you're gonna be one of my favorite characters." It just isn't there, it's like they made caricatures based off D&D and some MCU Collab Clorox they found in a shed.   Every single dialogue is awkward because of the silent protagonist. Why are people just talking at me, and I'm just reacting? I can't give a little "uh-huh" whenever I nod or something? Combined with the seven different dialogue choices that all do nothing except progress the dialogue, why should I care about the dialogue at all? Yeah the voice acting is cool, but if I know they'll just say the same thing anyway, I'll just pick number one, read, and skip. It's like Larian looked at all the bad rpg dialogue trees and said "Ok, so if we give them seven dialogue choices instead of five or three, then the illusion of choice will actually matter to the player." No it doesn't. I ordered RPG with my CRPG, like it comes with the order. I shouldn't have to sit here and beg on my hands and knees to play a different game than other people are playing. "Oh I've replayed Act 1 so many times, it's so reactive." Literally how, show me the reactivity. I played for almost 11 hours and the world didn't acknowledge my existence at all, and it was made all the more obvious by the fact that everyone else was acknowledged because they had a voice! It's all fake, you know what a fugazi is? I hate this illusion of choice bs, it's the quickest way to turn me off a game.  Also notice how nobody cared that I picked a Tiefling in the first quest? Here I am thinking "Maybe the Tieflings will be different to me because I'm a Tiefling, and I can cast Tiefling specific spells and help them more than I would in say, a dark elf playthrough." Lmao, why would I think there would be RPG elements in this game? Here's a couple random dialogue choices, they won't do anything different but maybe the character will mention tieflings in that response, then carry on with their original monologue. And they are monologues btw, I keep referring to them as dialogues but my party members were extremely silent the whole time.  And your character just stares. Everyone else is having a grand old time but me? I'm just there to react. Grandpa died? Sad face. Grandpa came back from the dead? Happy face. Tell me more in great detail about how your grandpa came back to life, I'd love to sit and react to your story while I click different buttons to make you say things.   Can we talk about the build variety I've been dying to talk about the build variety. What build variety? You get spellllllls, god I love spells. There's a scroll for this and a scroll for that, a spell for this and a spell for that. And you WILL be using the same party composition as everyone else is, because Astarion HAS to be a vampire rogue, and Lae'zel HAS to be a melee swordfighter. Which means your main character HAS to be viable or it throws the balance off, which means you get NO build variety. "Oh boy I levelled up, what do I get?" A stat increase, now clap. Maybe you got a spell or two. What am I even supposed to do with that, why should I care that my party members are levelling when they just get "more powerful +1".  Oh and Act 3 is still unfinished apparently? Why didn't they patch it later, or was it all that motion capture that was getting in the way?   The reality is whenever Larian got a license from Wizards of the Coast it was going to be terrible from the start. Of course WoTC is going to make them charge $60 constantly because it's Wizards of The Coast. Of course the ingame mod loader will disable achievements but downloading them from Nexus won't, it's WoTC and they're anti-competitive, and of course you will have Larian defenders blindly parrot "every other game does it with mods." Of course the game would be as dumbed down as possible, it's WoTC, they need to make it "palatable for modern audinces". Speaking of which, really crazy how they went with 5e and not 3.5e right? Such a little coinky-dink. Of course they have to make their money back for their really expensive motion capture and voice acting work. And, of course, there will be people that shut off their brain, plug their ears, and say "lah lah lah, I can't hear you." because they don't want to admit they didn't know what a CRPG even was before this took home every game of the year award in an objectively really mid year for video games.   If you care about voice acting, and motion capture, and beautiful graphics, and shallow hot characters, that's fine! But be honest about it, don't go touting your game around as God's gift to the earth because it has all those things. The constant $60 implies quality, and in those things I can find quality in. But I wanted a CRPG, not a motion capture simulator. This is the only review I have comments on, I'm not a hater by any means. In fact I have a lot of respect for Baldur's Gate, they were generational games that went on to inspire a lot of my favorite franchise. But this game is mediocre, and it sacrificed all the role-playing to focus more on voice acting and motion capture. If anyone wants to debate that with me here, that's fine. Again though, stop acting like your game is made out of solid gold, because it very much isn't.

Deeply disappointed.  I bought the game looking to play, after many years, a new Baldur's Gate game but got a different game; not a new one, I got a new Divinity Original Sin game.   Don't get me wrong, I really like the Divinity Original Sin series, and this game is a really good DOS game but is not Baldur's Gate and I cant stop feeling that I was tricked.   I wonder what happened here, I wonder if this was always the plan or if they tried and then decided to fall into a more familiar concept due to time/technical limitations. I don't know but is very disappointing.  I have been reading, searching, for opinions from people who were also feeling the same and that was also quite depressing because there are not that many and people who complain looks like are mostly talking about the combat system; like if instead of being turn based game was like the Baldur's gate games this would have been enough; its definitely not.  I mean, I'm not going to explain the characteristics of the Divinity Original Sin games, people who played them know what to expect and the same for Baldur's Gate games.  But this game was a huge success; well, is a great game (at least it looks like), and that's something that concerns me. It does because I feel that the chances to have a new (real) Baldur's Gate game are gone; if there is a 4th one will be the same as this one, why change? the game was critically acclaimed.  I believe I read people from Larian were big fans of the Baldur's Gate saga; I'm sure they have to be a bit surprised that not many people complained about this game... And probably there are not many fans of the original games that still play video games and the ones who played this game didn't played the previous DOS games so this one was an interesting discover.   Again, very disappointed, this was a complete display of cowardly and I think the person/people who made the decision to follow this approach should be, at least, a bit embarrassed.

Around the blighted village at level 3, I ran into a goblin that talked to me, initiated combat, and started it by throwing a grenade that downed all but one of my party members from full HP, and left the last one with 2HP. The party wiped, and 90 minute of progress with it.  If I DM'd an encounter like this for my friends, they would rightfully chew me out.  Instead of save scumming to finish poorly balanced encounters, go play 5E with your friends. Whoever you have run the game for you will have more common sense.  EDIT:  I managed to get to Act 3, and burnt out. My criticisms are largely the same. Skill and care was put into the storytelling, and the visuals are great, but the combats remain uncomfortably brutal. Customizing the settings to trivialize the combat and enjoy the storytelling might be the best way though this one, and I'd wait for a sale.  Also, the one running your actual D&D game should be YOU. YES, YOU. It's the most work, but entertaining your friends and putting them through your sick, twisted machinations is a unique privilege, and loads of fun.
